meta.description: Dark Elf origin story — the exile from House Vel'tharin

delf_001.title: The Warrens Below
text: You were born in darkness, and the darkness was kind.

The deep warrens of House Vel'tharin extended seven miles below the surface of the megacity — a labyrinth of obsidian halls, shadow-lit corridors, and ritual chambers carved from the living rock by generations of dark elves who chose the underground not as exile but as sovereignty. While the surface races scrambled for light and air and the illusion of permanence, your people built an empire in the dark. An empire of secrets, of whispered intelligence, of power that moved unseen through the foundations of every institution above.

You grew up knowing three truths. First: darkness is not absence. Darkness is presence — the presence of possibility, of hidden knowledge, of things that exist but cannot be seen. Second: information is the only currency that appreciates over time. Gold corrodes. Credits inflate. Data compounds. Third: the family you are born into will use you, sharpen you, deploy you, and — if you become inconvenient — dispose of you without the courtesy of a farewell.
House Vel'tharin taught you all three lessons. The third one, they taught last.

choices.text: Remember your childhood in the warrens

delf_002.title: Children of the Dark
text: Dark elf children are not raised. They are forged. From the age of five, every child in House Vel'tharin enters the Crucible — a decades-long program of education, combat training, and psychological conditioning designed to produce operatives of exceptional skill and absolute loyalty. The weak are not punished. They are simply... redirected. Assigned to lesser duties. Forgotten. In the warrens, mediocrity is its own death sentence.

Your cohort was twelve. By the time you were thirty, it was seven. The others didn't die — death is wasteful, and House Vel'tharin does not waste resources. They were reassigned to menial positions: maintenance of the lower tunnels, inventory management, the grinding administrative work that keeps an underground empire functioning. Alive, but erased from the bloodline records. Functionally invisible.

You survived the Crucible because you understood something the others didn't: the tests were never about strength or speed or cunning. They were about perception. The instructors weren't measuring what you could do. They were measuring what you could see. In the warrens, the one who sees the most wins. Always.

By your fortieth year, you could read a room's power dynamics in the time it took to blink. You could identify a lie by the micro-tension in a jaw muscle. You could map the emotional state of every person in a crowded hall by the way they held their shoulders. The Crucible didn't teach you to fight. It taught you to watch.

delf_003.title: The Specialization
text: At fifty, the Crucible assigns specializations. The instructors observe. The Matriarch decides. There is no appeal.

The three paths of House Vel'tharin: Blade, Shadow, or Sigil. Blades are the House's killers — assassins trained in seventeen forms of silent death, deployed against enemies who need to disappear without explanation. Shadows are the intelligence operatives — infiltrators who live double lives on the surface, gathering information that feeds the House's vast network. Sigils are the scholars — keepers of the forbidden texts, practitioners of the shadow arts, researchers of knowledge that the surface world has declared illegal or impossible.

You remember the day the Matriarch called your name. Vel'thara Syn — the Matriarch, your grandmother, a woman whose age was a state secret and whose eyes held the accumulated cruelty of centuries — sat on her throne of carved obsidian and studied you for a full minute without speaking. You stood before her and did not flinch, because flinching in the presence of the Matriarch was tantamount to resignation.

'You see too clearly for a Blade,' she said. 'Blades must be willing to stop seeing when told. You see too deeply for a Shadow. Shadows must maintain surfaces that your eyes would constantly undermine.' A pause. 'Sigil? No. The Sigils hide behind their books. You hide behind nothing. You are something we haven't produced in a generation.'

delf_004.title: The Siblings
text: In House Vel'tharin, 'sibling' is a strategic relationship, not a sentimental one. You shared blood with two others: Kael'ris, your elder by eleven years, and Veya, your younger by six. In the Crucible, blood ties were neither advantage nor liability. They were data points. The instructors watched how siblings interacted, noting loyalties, rivalries, dependencies — mapping the emotional architecture that could be exploited or reinforced as the House required.

Kael'ris was assigned Blade at forty-eight. He had the temperament for it — cold, precise, capable of compartmentalizing empathy with the efficiency of a surgeon removing an organ. You saw him kill for the first time when you were thirty-seven. A rival House's courier, intercepted in the tunnel junction between territories. Kael'ris did it with a needle through the base of the skull. The courier was dead before his knees buckled. Kael'ris caught the body to prevent the sound of its fall from echoing through the tunnels. Professional. Efficient. Empty.

Veya was assigned Shadow. She had the face for it — open, warm, a smile that made people want to trust her. In the warrens, that smile was a weapon more dangerous than any blade. On the surface, it was camouflage. Veya could become anyone. A corporate assistant, a street vendor, a charity worker. She wore identities like clothing and discarded them with the same indifference.
You loved them both, in the careful, measured way that dark elves love: with full awareness that love is a vulnerability, and vulnerabilities in House Vel'tharin are exploited.

delf_005.title: The Matriarch
text: Vel'thara Syn, Matriarch of House Vel'tharin, was the oldest living dark elf anyone could verify — though verification, in a culture built on secrets, was itself unreliable. Conservative estimates put her age at nine hundred. The less conservative whispered about rituals, about shadow-pacts, about transactions with entities that lived in the spaces between darkness and void.

She ruled House Vel'tharin for four centuries. Under her leadership, the House's intelligence network expanded from a local operation serving three rival Houses to a citywide web that sold information to corporations, governments, criminal organizations, and anyone else who could meet her price. Meridian bought from her. The city council bought from her. The underground syndicates bought from her. She sold to all sides and favored none, because favoritism implies loyalty, and the Matriarch's only loyalty was to the House.

You saw her smile once. It was during a negotiation with a corporate executive who had attempted to double-cross her. She smiled, spoke three sentences in the old dark tongue, and the executive's own security team turned on him. He was found floating in the industrial canal the next morning. No one investigated.

The Matriarch was not cruel. Cruelty implies emotion, and the Matriarch had refined herself beyond emotion centuries ago. She was efficient. She was inevitable. And she was, until the night everything changed, the closest thing you had to a god.

choices.text: Remember your role in the House

delf_006.title: The Shadow Rituals
text: The dark elves did not worship darkness. They used it. The shadow arts — a discipline older than the Houses, older than the warrens, possibly older than the dark elves themselves — were a technology dressed in the language of ritual. Manipulation of photonic fields. Redirection of ambient light. Exploitation of the gap between what is seen and what is real.

The First Shadow: concealment. Bending light around your body until you become invisible to optical sensors, organic and electronic alike. You learned this at twelve. By twenty, you could hold a full shadow-cloak for six hours without fatigue.

The Second Shadow: projection. Creating visual illusions — phantom images, false echoes, duplicates of yourself that moved and spoke while you stood elsewhere. More complex. You mastered it at thirty-five.

The Third Shadow: the Deep Dark. Reaching into the space between photons, the quantum gaps where light has not yet decided whether to exist, and drawing from it a darkness so absolute that it negates not just light but sound, heat, and electromagnetic radiation. A sphere of nothing. A hole in reality.
The Third Shadow was forbidden. Naturally, you learned it anyway. In House Vel'tharin, the forbidden was simply the curriculum for advanced students.

delf_007.title: The Weaver
text: The Matriarch called your specialization 'Weaver.' A role so rare that House Vel'tharin had not produced one in over a hundred years. Weavers combined the skills of all three paths — the Blade's lethality, the Shadow's deception, the Sigil's knowledge — into a single operative who answered directly to the Matriarch and no one else.

Your training was private. No cohort, no peers, no shared dormitories. The Matriarch herself supervised your education, an honor so unprecedented that it generated rumors through the warrens like shock waves through stone. Some said you were being groomed as her successor. Others said you were being prepared as a weapon against a threat only the Matriarch could see.
Both rumors, you would later learn, were partially correct.

For twenty years, the Matriarch taught you the arts that Blades, Shadows, and Sigils learned separately. But she also taught you something none of them knew: the Weaving. The ability to connect disparate pieces of information into patterns, to see the threads that linked seemingly unrelated events, to understand that the city above was not a collection of independent actors but a tapestry of interlocking interests that could be read — and manipulated — by anyone who learned to see the weave.
'The city is a loom,' she told you. 'Every person is a thread. Learn to read the pattern, and you control the fabric.'

delf_008.title: The Third Shadow
text: The first time you drew the Deep Dark, you were forty-three. Alone in the ritual chamber at the lowest level of the warrens, a room carved from a single block of obsidian that absorbed all ambient light. The chamber was already dark. Drawing the Third Shadow in a place of absolute darkness was like trying to deepen a well that had no bottom.

But you did it. You reached into the quantum gap between photons and pulled, and the darkness responded. Not as absence — as presence. The Deep Dark was alive, in a way that defied conventional physics. It had weight, texture, temperature. It wrapped around your fingers like liquid mercury and sank into your skin, and for a moment you existed in a space where the laws of the physical world were optional.

In that moment, you understood why the Third Shadow was forbidden. Not because it was dangerous — everything in House Vel'tharin was dangerous. Because it was addictive. The Deep Dark offered a kind of peace that the waking world could never match. In the absolute negation of light and sound and sensation, the constant pressure of the warrens — the politics, the surveillance, the exhausting performance of loyalty — simply ceased to exist.

You could have stayed. Part of you wanted to. The Deep Dark whispered in frequencies below hearing, offering oblivion dressed as tranquility. But you pulled yourself back, because the Matriarch had taught you that the most dangerous trap is the one you want to walk into.

choices.text: Remember your role in the House

delf_009.title: The First Weaving
text: Your first mission as Weaver was elegant in its complexity. Three rival Houses — Dre'vak, Syn'ari, and Mol'thas — had formed a secret alliance aimed at undermining House Vel'tharin's information monopoly. They planned to establish their own intelligence network on the surface, poaching Vel'tharin's contacts and clients.
The Matriarch gave you three weeks. You dismantled the alliance in eleven days.

Not through violence — a Blade could have assassinated the three House leaders, but that would have created martyrs and unified their followers. Not through exposure — a Shadow could have leaked the alliance to the other Houses, but that would have revealed Vel'tharin's intelligence capabilities. You used the Weaving. You identified the threads of self-interest that held the alliance together and pulled them apart.

A whispered rumor here: House Dre'vak was planning to betray Syn'ari once the network was established. A forged document there: House Mol'thas had been selling intelligence to Vel'tharin all along. A strategically intercepted message: the alliance's financial backer was actually a Vel'tharin front company.

By day eleven, the three Houses were at each other's throats. By day fifteen, they had dissolved the alliance and were individually approaching the Matriarch to sell out their former partners. The Matriarch accepted all three offers, because information from traitors is still information.
She was pleased. You could tell because she didn't reprimand you.

delf_010.title: The Night of Broken Threads
text: The warrens had been tense for months. You could feel it in the texture of the silence — the way conversations stopped when certain people entered rooms, the way the shadows in the corridors seemed to deepen, as though the darkness itself was holding its breath. Something was coming. Your Weaver's instincts read the patterns, but for the first time in your career, you couldn't identify the shape of the threat.

The Matriarch knew. She had to know — she knew everything that happened in the warrens, had known everything for four centuries. But she said nothing. She continued her routine: audiences in the morning, intelligence briefings at noon, private study in the evening. Her face, as always, revealed nothing.

It happened at the third bell of the deep night — the hour when even dark elf metabolism demands sleep. Explosions rocked the lower warrens. Not the crude chemical detonations of surface-world violence — these were shadow-charges, weapons forged from compressed Deep Dark energy that had been banned by inter-House treaty for a century.
Someone inside the House had armed themselves with forbidden weapons. Someone inside the House had decided that the Matriarch's four-hundred-year reign was over.

choices.text: Assess the situation from the shadows

delf_011.title: Years of Service
text: For thirty years, you served as the Matriarch's Weaver. Thirty years of pulling threads and watching patterns collapse and reform. You destabilized a corporate merger that would have threatened the House's information market. You identified and neutralized a mole inside the Sigil archives. You negotiated a trade agreement with a deep-warren mining collective that secured the House's mineral supply for a century.

Each success deepened your understanding of the Weave. The city above was not chaotic — it was a system of staggering complexity, and every system had patterns. Patterns could be read. Patterns could be predicted. And patterns, once understood, could be shaped.

But you also began to see something the Matriarch didn't want you to see: the pattern of the House itself. The Vel'tharin intelligence network — the empire she had built over four centuries — was showing stress fractures. Information was commoditizing. Other Houses were developing their own surface networks. The monopoly was eroding, and with it, the House's power.

More troublingly, you noticed a pattern within the Matriarch's own behavior. Small inconsistencies. Decisions that prioritized short-term control over long-term stability. Meetings with individuals who didn't appear in the House records. A Weaver sees threads that others miss, and some of those threads led to conclusions you didn't want to draw.
The Matriarch was planning something. And she was planning it without you.

delf_012.title: The Matriarch's Chamber
text: You run through corridors lit by the strobe of shadow-charge detonations, the Deep Dark energy crackling through the warrens like black lightning. Bodies litter the hallways — Vel'tharin guards, caught mid-shift by the attack. Their wounds are strange: not cuts or burns, but voids. Places where the Deep Dark has simply erased flesh, leaving clean-edged absences in the shape of missing limbs and torsos.

The Matriarch's chamber is at the center of the warrens, behind three rings of security that should be impenetrable. The outer ring is already breached — the guards dead, the wards dissolved. The middle ring holds, but barely. Shadow-charges impact the obsidian walls with percussive silence, each one eating a little more of the stone.

You reach the inner sanctum. The Matriarch stands at the center, and for the first time in your life, you see her afraid. Not of death — Vel'thara Syn has stared down death for centuries. She is afraid of something worse. She is afraid of irrelevance.
'It's Kael'ris,' she says, and the name drops like a stone into water. Your brother. Your Blade. 'He's made alliance with House Dre'vak. Half the House has turned with him. He wants the throne.'

Your brother. Your blood. The boy you grew up with in the Crucible, the young man whose first kill you witnessed, the Blade who answered to the Matriarch with the same absolute loyalty you did. Until he didn't.

choices.text: "Tell me everything. I'll weave a response."

delf_013.title: Blood First
text: Instinct overrides training. Before you analyze, before you strategize, before you do any of the things the Matriarch spent decades teaching you, you move toward Kael'ris's quarters. Blood first. Strategy after.

His quarters are empty. The door is open, which is wrong — Blades never leave doors open. The room has been cleared with the methodical efficiency of someone who doesn't plan to return. Weapons gone. Personal items gone. The only thing left is a small obsidian figurine on the desk — a chess piece, the assassin, the most powerful attacking piece in the dark elf variant of the game. It's facing your chair.

A message. Kael'ris is telling you that he's the threat, and he's left the message where he knows you'll look first. He predicted you. He knows you'll come for blood before strategy. He's using your love against you.

Cold recognition: your brother is not acting impulsively. This coup has been planned for months, maybe years. And he's accounted for you in his calculations. The figurine is proof: he expects you to come, and he's already positioned his counter.
You pick up the figurine and squeeze it until the obsidian edges cut your palm. Then you go to find the Matriarch, because blood first was a mistake, and now you need to catch up.

delf_014.title: Reading the Chaos
text: The Weaver's first instinct: observe. You draw the First Shadow around yourself and vanish into the corridor's darkness, invisible even to dark elf eyes. From the shadows, you watch the chaos unfold and you read it the way the Matriarch taught you to read everything: as a pattern.

The attack is coordinated — three simultaneous strikes on the barracks, the archives, and the Matriarch's outer defenses. Military precision. This isn't a spontaneous uprising; it's a planned operation with clear objectives and a chain of command. The attackers wear no House insignia, but their combat style is unmistakable: Blade-school. These are Vel'tharin-trained assassins fighting against their own House.

You count the attackers. Thirty-seven visible in the corridors. That's roughly half the House's Blade contingent. A fifty-fifty split means the coup has been building for a long time — you don't turn half a military force overnight.

You identify the command structure: three Blade lieutenants coordinating the assault via shadow-pulse communication. Their signals converge on a single source. The source is in the eastern corridor — Kael'ris's command post.
Your brother. Your Blade. Leading a coup against the Matriarch with half the House at his back.
You move through the shadows toward the Matriarch's chamber, because a Weaver's place in a crisis is beside the person who holds the most threads.

delf_015.title: The Investigation
text: You investigated, because that's what Weavers do. Carefully, invisibly, with the patient methodology of someone who has spent decades learning to see what others hide. You pulled the threads of the Matriarch's anomalous behavior and followed them into the dark.

What you found was a partnership. The Matriarch — the architect of independence, the sovereign of the deep warrens, the dark elf who had built an empire on the principle that House Vel'tharin owed allegiance to no surface entity — had entered a secret agreement with Meridian Corporation. She was selling them something she had never sold before: not information about others, but information about the House itself. Dark elf biology. Shadow art methodology. The genetic basis of the deep-dark affinity.

Meridian was paying a fortune. The Matriarch was funneling the credits into a private fund that even the House's financial officers couldn't trace. And the purpose of the fund, as far as you could determine, was the construction of a second warren — a backup House, hidden in the deep tunnels, staffed by a skeleton crew of loyalists who would survive if the primary warrens fell.

The Matriarch was planning for the end of House Vel'tharin. Not preventing it. Planning for it. She had looked at the same patterns you'd seen — the eroding monopoly, the rising competition — and concluded that the House was doomed. So she was selling its secrets to fund an escape.
Your god had feet of clay. And the clay was made of corporate credits.

choices.text: Act on the investigation. Patience has a cost.

delf_016.title: The Confrontation
text: You confronted her in her private study — a room you had entered perhaps a dozen times in thirty years, always by invitation, never by demand. This time, you didn't wait for an invitation. You walked through the door, sat down across from her, and placed a single data crystal on the obsidian desk between you. The crystal contained everything you'd found: the Meridian agreement, the financial transfers, the second-warren construction records.

The Matriarch looked at the crystal for a long time. Then she looked at you. Her expression didn't change — it never changed — but something shifted in her eyes. Not fear. Not anger. Recognition. The recognition of a predator who has been spotted by another predator.
'You are exactly what I made you,' she said. 'A Weaver who sees every thread, including the ones I tried to hide. I should have anticipated this.'
'You sold us,' you said. 'You sold our biology, our arts, our identity. To Meridian.'

'I secured our survival,' she corrected. 'The House is dying. The intelligence monopoly is broken. In twenty years, we'll be just another warren of dark elves selling low-grade information to mid-level clients. I am ensuring that when the collapse comes, there is something left. A seed. A remnant. A thread to weave a new pattern from.'
She said the word 'seed' without irony, without awareness of the echo. But you heard it. And you understood that the Matriarch was not evil. She was desperate. And desperate people sell anything.

choices.text: [Strength 11] The confrontation taught you force has its place.

delf_017.title: The Weaver's Response
text: The Matriarch gives you what she has: the disposition of forces, the identities of the turned Blades, the intelligence on Kael'ris's alliance with House Dre'vak. You process it in the Weaver's way — not as a list of facts but as a pattern of relationships. Every alliance has seams. Every coalition has members whose commitment is conditional. Find the conditions, and you find the leverage.

Kael'ris's coup has a critical weakness: House Dre'vak's involvement. The Dre'vak forces are professional, but they're outsiders in Vel'tharin territory. They don't know the warrens the way native Vel'tharin do. And they're here for profit, not ideology — Kael'ris promised them access to the intelligence network, but if the network is compromised, their motivation evaporates.

'I need access to the communications array,' you tell the Matriarch. 'If I can broadcast the right signal at the right time, I can split Dre'vak from Kael'ris. Turn the coup into a three-way conflict. In the chaos, we consolidate.'
The Matriarch studies you. 'And Kael'ris? Your brother?'

The question hangs in the dark air between you. You know what she's asking. She's asking if you can do to your own brother what you've done to a dozen rival Houses: pull his threads until his pattern collapses.

choices.text: "He made his choice. I'll make mine."
"I'll stop the coup. But I won't kill him."

delf_018.title: Veya's Shadow
text: 'Veya is not in the warrens,' the Matriarch says, and her voice carries an inflection you've never heard before — something that, in a lesser being, you would call guilt. 'I sent her to the surface three days ago. A deep-cover assignment. She doesn't know about the coup.'

Three days. The Matriarch sent Veya away three days before the attack. Either the timing is coincidence — and the Matriarch does not believe in coincidence — or she knew the attack was coming and removed Veya from the danger zone. Which means she knew about Kael'ris. Which means she allowed it to happen.

The realization hits you with the force of a shadow-charge. The Matriarch knew. She could have prevented the coup. Instead, she positioned assets — sent Veya out, kept you close — and let Kael'ris make his move. Why?

Because a coup attempt, once exposed, gives the Matriarch justification for a purge. Every rival, every dissenter, every person who ever looked at the obsidian throne with ambition can be swept away in the cleanup. Kael'ris isn't threatening the Matriarch's power. He's providing her an excuse to consolidate it.
Your brother is a pawn. And you — loyal, obedient, standing in the Matriarch's chamber ready to defend her — are the instrument she'll use to remove him.

choices.text: Set aside the revelation — deal with the coup first

delf_019.title: Searching for Veya
text: Veya's quarters are intact — no signs of violence. Her personal effects are in order, but her field kit is gone. She's not in the warrens. In the middle of a coup that's tearing the House apart, your sister is simply absent.

You pull up her assignment log on a terminal that's still functioning. Last entry: three days ago. 'Surface deployment — classified. Duration: indefinite. Authorization: Matriarch.' The Matriarch sent her away. Before the coup. Before any of this.

A deep-cover Shadow operative on the surface, unaware that her House is burning. If the coup succeeds, Veya becomes a fugitive — an operative without a handler, a Shadow with no House to cast her. If the Matriarch wins, Veya is safe but trapped in an assignment she can't abort.
Either way, Veya is beyond your reach. You can't help her from here. The only way to protect her is to influence the outcome of the battle raging through the warrens.
You close the terminal and head for the Matriarch's chamber. The fastest way to help your sister is to end this coup. One way or another.

choices.text: Search for Veya first. The Matriarch can wait.

delf_020.title: The Counter-Weave
text: You reach the communications array through a maintenance shaft that only the Matriarch and her Weaver know about. The array is the nervous system of the House — a network of shadow-pulse transmitters that allow instantaneous encrypted communication throughout the warrens. Control the array, and you control the flow of information. Control information, and you control everything.
Your fingers move across the console with the precision of decades of training. You compose three messages, each calibrated for its audience with the care of a jeweler cutting a diamond.

To House Dre'vak's commander: a falsified intelligence report suggesting that Kael'ris has already negotiated a separate deal with the Matriarch and plans to betray Dre'vak once they've done the heavy fighting. Plausible, because it's exactly what a dark elf would do.

To the uncommitted Vel'tharin personnel — the fifty percent who haven't joined either side: a message from the Matriarch offering full amnesty, plus a promotion, to anyone who returns to loyal service within the hour. Self-interest is the most reliable lever.
To Kael'ris: nothing. Silence is its own message. It tells him that someone has reached the array before him, that his communications are compromised, that the Weaver is awake and working.

delf_021.title: The Line
text: The Matriarch's expression doesn't change. But something in the air between you crystallizes. You've drawn a line. In House Vel'tharin, lines are luxuries that operatives cannot afford.
'Sentiment,' she says, and the word carries the weight of a death sentence. 'I trained you to see all threads. Sentiment blinds you to the threads that connect you to your enemies.'
'He's my brother. Not my enemy.'

'He is currently directing the assault that is killing our people.' Her voice is ice. 'He has allied with House Dre'vak against his own blood. He has armed himself with forbidden weapons. And you — my Weaver, my instrument, the most capable operative this House has produced in a century — you wish to spare him because you shared a womb.'

You meet her gaze. 'I'll stop the coup. I'll break his alliance. I'll strip him of power and support and leave him nowhere to run. But I will not be the instrument of my brother's death. If that's what you require, find another Weaver.'

The silence stretches. Then: 'Stop the coup. We'll discuss Kael'ris's fate afterward.' It's not agreement. It's deferral. In the Matriarch's vocabulary, that's the closest thing to a concession you'll ever get.

choices.text: Cross the line. Some weaves require sacrifice.

delf_022.title: The Matriarch Exposed
text: The Matriarch doesn't deny it. She never denies — denial implies vulnerability. Instead, she inclines her head, the most minimal acknowledgment possible, and says: 'Kael'ris needed to reveal himself. His ambition was a cancer in the House — growing, metastasizing, eventually fatal. I allowed it to surface so it could be excised.'
'You're using your own grandson as bait.'

'I'm using an enemy of the House who happens to share my bloodline. The distinction matters.' Her eyes are obsidian mirrors. 'I did not create his ambition. I did not arm him with shadow-charges or forge his alliance with Dre'vak. I merely chose not to stop him prematurely. A surgeon doesn't stop a tumor from growing — she waits until it's visible, then she cuts.'

You stare at her and see, for the first time, the full architecture of her power. Four centuries of rule, maintained not through strength or wisdom but through an absolute willingness to sacrifice anything — including her own family — to preserve the structure of the House. She is the House. The House is her. Everything else — children, grandchildren, alliances, principles — is thread to be woven or cut as the pattern demands.
And you are her Weaver. Her instrument. And she is asking you to cut your own brother.

choices.text: Comply — stop the coup, deal with the ethics later

delf_023.title: The Unraveling
text: The counter-weave works with the precision of a scalpel. House Dre'vak's commander receives the falsified report and, in the paranoid calculus of dark elf politics, believes it immediately. Within twenty minutes, the Dre'vak forces pull back from the main assault and begin securing their own escape routes. Kael'ris's western flank collapses.

The amnesty offer splits the uncommitted fifty-fifty. Some return to the Matriarch's side. Others hesitate, trying to read the shifting power dynamics before committing. The hesitation is enough — it creates pockets of inaction that the Matriarch's loyalists exploit, securing key junctions and chokepoints while the hesitators dither.

Kael'ris, to his credit, adapts quickly. He recognizes the counter-weave for what it is — he knows your work, after all, grew up watching you practice it — and consolidates his remaining forces in the eastern corridor. He's retreating, but he's retreating in good order, maintaining unit cohesion, protecting his core loyalists.

The coup is broken. The House is bleeding but intact. And your brother is cornered in the eastern tunnels with thirty loyal Blades, nowhere to go, and the full weight of the Matriarch's fury descending.
The Matriarch turns to you and says: 'Finish it.'

delf_024.title: The Refusal
text: 'No.'

The word echoes in the Matriarch's chamber with a finality that surprises even you. It's a word that hasn't been spoken to Vel'thara Syn in four centuries. It hangs in the air between you like a blade.

The Matriarch's eyes go flat. Not angry — beyond angry. The calm that follows when a machine encounters input it was not designed to process. You've seen that look directed at enemies, at traitors, at corporate executives who miscalculated their leverage. You've never seen it directed at you.
'You refuse,' she says. Not a question.

'You orchestrated this. You let Kael'ris build his coup so you could use it as justification for a purge. You sent Veya away to protect her — not because you love her, but because she's a useful asset. And now you want me to execute my own brother so your hands stay clean.' You meet her gaze without flinching. 'No.'

'Then you are no longer my Weaver.' The Matriarch's voice carries no inflection. 'You are no longer Vel'tharin. You are exiled. From this moment, you have no House, no name, no bloodline. You are nothing.'
And like that, eighty years of life, training, identity, and purpose are severed. One word. One refusal. And you are cast into the void.

choices.text: Try to reach Kael'ris before they do

delf_025.title: Brother's Edge
text: You find Kael'ris in the eastern corridor, behind a barricade of overturned furniture and obsidian slabs. His remaining Blades — twenty-three, you count, not thirty — form a defensive perimeter with the disciplined efficiency of soldiers who know they've lost but haven't accepted it yet.

Kael'ris sees you approach. The First Shadow doesn't work on someone who trained alongside you in the Crucible — he knows your concealment patterns, your movement signatures, the particular way you breathe when you're cloaked. He drops his weapon and walks forward to meet you in the no-man's-land between his barricade and the Matriarch's advance.
'Weaver,' he says. The title, not your name. The professional designation. He's already made you an instrument rather than a sibling.
'Blade,' you respond. Two can play that game.

He smiles. It's the first genuine expression you've seen on his face in years. 'She sent you to finish it. Of course she did. The Matriarch uses the cleanest blade, and you were always the sharpest thing in the warrens.'
'She wants you dead. I'm here to offer an alternative.'

His smile doesn't waver. 'There is no alternative. I tried to take the throne. I failed. In the warrens, failure has one consequence.' He draws a secondary blade — small, personal, not a weapon of war but a weapon of principle. 'The question is: who delivers it?'

choices.text: "Put the blade down. I can get you out."
Draw your own blade — meet him as an equal

delf_026.title: Cast Out
text: Exile in dark elf culture is absolute. They don't simply remove you from the roster. They erase you. Your name is struck from the bloodline records. Your quarters are sealed and reassigned. Your contributions to the House — thirty years of Weaving, every mission, every success — are attributed to 'anonymous' in the official histories. You become a non-person, a ghost, a hole in the pattern where a thread used to be.

They give you one hour to leave. The Matriarch's guards escort you to the surface exit — a concealed door in a maintenance corridor beneath the Transit Hub — and they stand in silence as you step through. The last guard, a woman named Tyrel who you trained with in the Crucible, meets your eyes for a fraction of a second before the door closes. In that fraction, you see something unexpected: pity.

The door seals behind you with the finality of a coffin lid. On one side: the only world you've ever known, the labyrinth of shadows that shaped every aspect of your being. On the other: the surface. Light. Air. The incomprehensible chaos of a city that operates in dimensions you've spent your entire life observing from below but never actually inhabiting.

You stand in a maintenance corridor beneath the Transit Hub, blinking against the dim emergency lighting that your dark-adapted eyes register as blazingly bright, and you realize that you have nothing. No House. No name. No identity. No purpose. Just the skills the Matriarch gave you, applied to a world you've only ever studied from the shadows.

delf_027.title: The Offer
text: 'Out?' Kael'ris laughs, and it's the bitter laugh of someone who understands the architecture of their own cage. 'Out where? The warrens are the only world we know. The surface is — you've seen the intelligence reports. It's chaos up there. Noise and light and people who don't understand the first thing about shadow or subtlety.'
'Better than dying in a corridor for the Matriarch's ego.'

His laughter stops. He looks at you, really looks, and for a moment the Blade facade drops and you see the boy from the Crucible. The boy who killed a courier at thirty-seven and caught the body so it wouldn't make a sound. The boy who did what the House required, always, without question — until the questions got too loud to ignore.

'She sold us,' he says quietly. 'You know that, don't you? The Meridian deal. Our biology, our arts, our secrets — she sold them all. I found the records six months ago. That's why I moved against her. Not ambition. Not power. Because the Matriarch is dismantling the House from the inside and selling the pieces to a corporation.'
You go still. He knows. Kael'ris found the same thread you did — the Matriarch's secret deal with Meridian. But where you investigated, he acted. Where you sought to understand, he sought to stop it.

choices.text: "I know about the deal. I found it too."
"It doesn't matter. We need to leave, now."

delf_028.title: The Brother's Reason
text: Kael'ris is quiet for a long time. Behind his barricade, his Blades watch the corridor with the focused stillness of predators. In front of him, you stand unarmed, unarmored, presenting yourself as a target or a bridge. The distance between the two is one decision.

'I found it by accident,' he says. 'Three months ago. A financial transfer that didn't route through the House's normal channels. I followed it because that's what Blades do when something doesn't smell right — we follow it until we find the source and then we cut it out.'

'The source was the Matriarch. She's been selling our genetic data to Meridian for two years. Not just intelligence — our actual biology. The shadow-affinity sequences, the night-vision adaptations, the neural architecture that allows shadow manipulation. She's selling the blueprints of what makes us us.'

He grips the blade so hard his knuckles whiten. 'I went to her. I confronted her. And you know what she said? She said it was necessary. That the House was dying and this was the only way to preserve a remnant. She asked me to accept it. To be complicit.'
'I am a Blade. I was forged to protect the House. I could not protect it from the Matriarch. So I tried to remove the Matriarch.' He pauses. 'I failed. But at least I tried.'

choices.text: "We both saw it. She used us both."

delf_029.title: Blade to Blade
text: You draw your blade — not the monofilament, not a weapon of technology, but the obsidian knife that every Vel'tharin carries from the day they complete the Crucible. It's a symbol as much as a weapon: dark elf to dark elf, honor in the old way, before the Houses and the politics and the centuries of accumulated treachery.

Kael'ris nods. Understanding passes between you without words. This is not about killing. This is about resolution. In the old dark elf tradition — older than the Houses, from the time when dark elves were a single people in the deep places — disputes between blood-kin were settled blade to blade. Not to the death, but to the yielding. The one who yields accepts the other's judgment.

He moves first. Blade-school training, flawless and fast. You counter with the Weaver's art — reading his pattern, predicting his line, placing yourself where his blade isn't going to be. He's faster. You're smarter. The exchange lasts ninety seconds — an eternity in blade-work — before you find the opening.

Your knife at his throat. His knife at your ribs. Perfect stalemate. Two siblings, forged in the same Crucible, trained by the same House, arriving at the same conclusion by different paths: the Matriarch must be stopped.
'Yield?' you ask.
'Yield,' he says. And then: 'Together?'
'Together.'

choices.text: Plan your escape from the warrens
[Strength 11] Fight through rather than plan around.

delf_030.title: Shared Knowledge
text: You tell him what you know. The Meridian deal, the second warren, the Matriarch's escape plan. He fills in the gaps with intelligence you didn't have: the specific Meridian contacts, the timeline of data transfers, the identities of the House members who knowingly participated in the sell-out.
Together, the picture is complete. And it's worse than either of you understood alone.

Meridian isn't just buying dark elf biology. They're building something: a synthetic dark elf. A laboratory-created organism with the shadow affinity, the night vision, the neural architecture — but without the inconvenient free will. A controllable shadow operative. A corporate assassin that can do everything a dark elf can do, except refuse orders.
'Project designation: Eclipse,' Kael'ris says. 'They've been running it for eighteen months. The Matriarch provided the genetic templates. They're close to a prototype.'

The implications cascade through your mind like shadow-charges through a corridor. If Meridian creates synthetic dark elves, the real dark elves become obsolete. The intelligence networks, the shadow arts, the centuries of accumulated knowledge — all replaceable by a product line. The Matriarch isn't selling the House's future. She's selling the species.
'We can't stop this from inside the warrens,' you say. 'We need to go above.'

choices.text: [Intelligence 10] Plan every detail. Leave nothing to chance.

delf_031.title: The Escape
text: The escape route is one you mapped years ago — a Weaver's contingency, never formally logged in House records. A narrow passage that descends below the warrens into the pre-city cave system, then angles upward through a disused utility shaft to emerge in the Scrap Yards on the surface.

Kael'ris sends his remaining Blades into the deep tunnels — scattering, dispersing, making pursuit impossible. A handful volunteer to come with you. You take three: Tyrel, the guard who showed you pity at exile; Ven, a young Sigil with expertise in data systems; and Mira, a Blade whose loyalty to Kael'ris is absolute.

You leave the warrens in the dark, moving through passages that haven't been traveled in decades. The stone is rough, uncarved, natural. No obsidian floors, no shadow-lit halls. Just raw earth and the sound of your own breathing.

At the point where the warrens end and the wild tunnels begin, Kael'ris stops. He turns and looks back at the darkness that leads to everything you both grew up knowing. Then he turns forward, toward the darkness that leads to everything you don't.
'The Matriarch told me once that the strong adapt and the weak remember,' he says. 'I think she had it backward.'
You walk into the unknown. You do not look back.

choices.text: [Dexterity 11] Move through the tunnels like a shadow.

delf_032.title: Alone in the Tunnels
text: If you left without Kael'ris — if exile came alone — the tunnels are a different kind of darkness. Not the cultivated shadow of the warrens, designed for comfort and control. This is the raw dark, the primordial absence of light that existed before the dark elves claimed it. In this darkness, you are not sovereign. You are just another animal in the black.

You move by touch and memory, following the contours of the tunnel wall with fingers trained to read stone the way a surface dweller reads text. The route to the surface takes four hours. In those four hours, you have nothing but your thoughts, and your thoughts are merciless.

Everything you are — Weaver, operative, dark elf — was defined by the House. Without the House, the skills remain, but the identity collapses. You can see patterns, read power dynamics, manipulate threads. But for whom? For what purpose? A tool without a hand to wield it is just an object lying on the ground.

By the time you reach the surface exit, you have an answer. Not a complete one — not the kind of strategic clarity the Matriarch demanded — but a direction. You will use what you know to stop what the Matriarch has started. Project Eclipse cannot be allowed to succeed. The dark elf species — flawed, fractured, dysfunctional — is still yours. And you will defend it.

choices.text: Trust the darkness. It raised you.

delf_033.title: The Burning World
text: The surface is an assault on every sense you possess. Light — even the dim, filtered, acid-rain-dampened light of the Lower District — hits your dark-adapted eyes like a weapon. You flinch, draw the First Shadow instinctively, and the concealment field dulls the photonic input enough for your pupils to adjust. It takes fifteen minutes before you can open your eyes fully, and even then the world looks overexposed, washed out, drained of the rich detail that darkness provides.

Sound is worse. The warrens were engineered for acoustic control — every surface calculated to absorb or redirect sound in useful ways. The surface is sonic chaos. Machinery. Voices. Music. Rain on metal. Drone engines. The constant, grinding, omnipresent hum of a city that never sleeps and never shuts up.

And the space. The vast, horrifying openness of streets and corridors that extend beyond what your tunnel-calibrated spatial awareness can comfortably process. You keep your back to walls. You flinch at open doorways. Every intersection feels like a kill zone.

This is the world you've observed from below for decades. The world your Shadows infiltrated, your Blades operated in, your intelligence network mapped and cataloged. You knew everything about it except what it felt like to stand in it without a House behind you.

choices.text: Find shelter — you need time to adapt
Use your skills — assess the environment

delf_034.title: Searching for Veya
text: Veya's deep-cover assignment is classified at a level that even your Weaver clearance couldn't fully access. But you know the protocols. A Shadow on deep cover maintains a series of dead drops — physical locations where coded messages can be left for handlers. The locations rotate on a schedule that the Shadow memorizes before deployment.

You know Veya's rotation. You taught her the mnemonic system she uses to remember the sequence. It takes you three hours of navigating the Lower District's bewildering surface geography, but you find the first dead drop: a magnetic clip behind a ventilation grate in a transit corridor near Sector 5.

You leave a message in the family cipher — the private code that you, Kael'ris, and Veya developed as children in the Crucible, before the House taught you to trust no one. Three words: 'Threads cut. Surface. Meet.'

You wait. For two days, you wait — sleeping in a maintenance alcove, eating nothing, watching the dead drop from a shadow-cloak concealment point. On the third morning, a message appears in the magnetic clip. Same cipher.
'Neon Strip. The Glass Eye. Ask for Sera.' Veya's handwriting. Veya's code. Veya's alive.

choices.text: Veya may not want to be found. Go to the Glass Eye anyway.

delf_035.title: Shelter
text: You find shelter in the most obvious place: the underground. Not the warrens — those are closed to you now, sealed and hostile. But the city has its own underground: maintenance tunnels, abandoned subway lines, the connective tissue of infrastructure that surface dwellers forget exists. For a dark elf, these spaces are almost comfortable. The ceilings are low, the lighting is dim, and the acoustic profile is manageable.

You establish a base in a dead-end maintenance corridor in Sector 7, behind a door that hasn't been opened in years. The space is small — six by ten feet — but it has functioning power, a water tap, and most importantly, darkness. You draw the First Shadow around the doorway, rendering it invisible to casual observation, and you collapse.

Sleep comes in fragments. Your body is adjusting to a new circadian rhythm — or rather, it's abandoning the deep-warren schedule it's maintained for eighty years and trying to synchronize with the surface world's day-night cycle. Your dreams are full of obsidian corridors and the Matriarch's flat, ancient eyes.

When you wake, you assess your resources. Skills: extensive. Contacts: zero. Currency: none. Identity: erased. Equipment: one obsidian blade, the clothes on your back, and eighty years of training from the most sophisticated intelligence operation on the planet.
You've started from nothing before. The Crucible began with nothing. This is just another Crucible.

delf_036.title: The Weaver's Eye
text: The sensory overload passes. The Weaver's training kicks in. And the surface world, which moments ago was incomprehensible noise, resolves into a pattern so complex and so beautiful that you nearly forget to breathe.

Every person on the street is a thread. The vendor haggling over prices — her body language says she's in debt and desperate. The patrol drone overhead — its scanning pattern has a three-second gap between sweeps. The two men in the alley — not muggers, runners, watching for a delivery. The woman in the window of the building across the street — augmented eyes, recording everything, corporate surveillance disguised as residential.

You read the Lower District in ninety seconds flat. Power dynamics, economic pressures, information flows, security architecture — the same analysis you'd apply to a rival House's warrens, applied to a city block. The scale is different but the principles are identical. Every system is a weave. Every weave has patterns. Every pattern has threads you can pull.

The city is not chaos. The city is the most complex weave you've ever encountered, and you are a Weaver without a House, which means you are free to read it without obligation and act on what you see without permission.
For the first time since the exile, something that feels like excitement stirs in your chest.

choices.text: Find Veya — she's the first thread

delf_037.title: The Glass Eye
text: The Neon Strip is the Lower District's entertainment corridor — a sensory overload of holographic advertisements, music venues, gambling dens, and establishments that cater to every appetite the city has cultivated and commodified. For a dark elf fresh from the warrens, it's like being thrown into a reactor. Light everywhere. Sound everywhere. Sensation battering every surface of your body.

The Glass Eye is a bar near the Strip's eastern end, identifiable by a holographic eye above the door that blinks in slow, unsettling rhythm. Inside, the lighting is mercifully dim — the kind of calculated darkness that the surface world uses for ambiance, a pale imitation of the real thing, but enough to let your eyes relax.
You ask for Sera at the bar. The bartender — a large orc with cybernetic hands — nods toward a booth in the back corner. Dark, isolated, sight lines to every exit. A Shadow's chosen seat.

Veya sits in the booth with a drink she isn't drinking and a face she's wearing that isn't hers. It takes you a moment to see past the surface persona — the hair color, the posture, the slight alteration to her facial structure that dark elf Shadow art can achieve. But her eyes are her own. Dark violet, sharp, the eyes of someone who's been wearing other people's faces for so long she's not sure which one is real anymore.
'Hello, sibling,' she says. 'You look terrible.'

choices.text: "You don't know what happened, do you?"

delf_038.title: Building from Nothing
text: The first week is survival. You learn the Lower District's economy the same way you learned the warrens — by observation, pattern recognition, and the judicious application of skills that your new neighbors don't know you have.

Information is still currency. On the surface, it's less refined — not the surgical intelligence-trading of the Houses, but the raw, street-level exchange of who's selling what, who's in debt to whom, which patrol routes have gaps. You sell what you see. A vendor in the Market District pays you twenty credits for a tip about a competitor's supply schedule. A runner in the tunnels pays thirty for a map of patrol drone patterns you compiled in a single afternoon of observation.

Your shadow arts give you advantages no surface dweller can match. The First Shadow makes you invisible to the drones. Your dark-adapted vision lets you navigate the maintenance tunnels without light, accessing areas that other tunnel runners need expensive equipment to reach. Your ability to read body language and social dynamics makes you an uncanny negotiator.
Within a month, you have a reputation in the Lower District's informal economy: the dark elf who sees everything and says very little. It's not a House. It's not an empire. But it's a start.

And all the while, beneath the work of survival, you're pulling threads. Mapping the surface world's power structures. Tracking Meridian's activities. Building the intelligence picture you'll need to stop Project Eclipse.

delf_039.title: The Full Thread
text: You tell Veya everything. The coup. The Matriarch's manipulation. Kael'ris's discovery of the Meridian deal. The exile. Project Eclipse. You lay every thread on the table between you, and you watch her face as the pattern forms.

Veya is Shadow-trained, which means her face reveals exactly what she wants it to reveal. But you're her sibling, her blood, and you can read the micro-expressions that her training doesn't quite suppress. Shock — quickly controlled. Anger — channeled into the rigid set of her jaw. Grief — buried so deep it only surfaces as a slight widening of her pupils when you describe the exile.

'She sent me away,' Veya says. 'Three days before the coup. A deep-cover assignment that she knew would take me off the board.' Her voice is flat, analytical, the Shadow processing intelligence. But her hands, beneath the table where she thinks you can't see, are shaking.
'She protected you.'
'She removed me as a variable. There's a difference.' Veya drains her untouched drink in one swallow. 'Kael'ris — is he alive?'
You tell her what you know. Kael'ris alive, scattered into the deep tunnels with his remaining loyalists. Or Kael'ris still in the warrens, fate unknown. The truth depends on the path you walked.
'We need to move,' Veya says. 'If the Matriarch exiled you, she's watching for you. And if she's watching for you, she's watching for me. We need to go dark. Darker than the warrens.'

choices.text: "I have a plan. But first, Eclipse."
"Where do we go? Who can we trust?"

delf_040.title: Veya in the Dark
text: Veya's Shadow-mask flickers. For a fraction of a second, you see raw confusion beneath the professional exterior. She's been on deep cover for days, maintaining a surface persona while running a standard intelligence-gathering operation. She doesn't know that the House she's gathering intelligence for no longer exists — at least, not in the form she left it.

'I lost comm contact two days ago,' she says. 'My handler went dark. I assumed it was a security protocol — deep cover sometimes requires communication blackout. I've been maintaining cover and waiting for reactivation.' She pauses. 'What happened?'
You tell her. Measured, precise, in the clinical language of an intelligence briefing. Coup. Matriarch's manipulation. Meridian deal. Exile.

Veya listens without interruption. When you finish, she sits in silence for a long time. The bar's ambient noise fills the space between you — music, laughter, the clink of glasses. Sounds from a world that doesn't know or care that a dark elf's entire reality just collapsed.
'The Matriarch sent me away to protect me,' Veya says finally. 'Or to remove a complication. I'm not sure which. I'm not sure it matters.' She looks at you. 'What do we do now?'
'We do what we've always done,' you say. 'We adapt. We survive. And we stop what needs stopping.'

choices.text: "First, we need a base. Somewhere safe."

delf_041.title: Threading Eclipse
text: Project Eclipse. Meridian's program to create synthetic dark elves using genetic templates provided by the Matriarch. If it succeeds, the real dark elves become obsolete — replaced by a corporate product line with the same abilities and none of the inconvenient independence.

You begin pulling threads. The Weaver's art, applied to the surface world for the first time without the House's resources or the Matriarch's direction. It's harder. The surface world's information architecture is different — less structured than the House system, more chaotic, but also more porous. Gaps and leaks that a warren-trained operative can exploit.

Meridian's public-facing records reveal nothing — the corporation keeps its classified programs behind encryption that would take years to crack through conventional means. But the supply chain is vulnerable. Every secret project needs resources, and resources leave trails.

You map Eclipse's supply chain through its commercial footprint: purchases of genetic sequencing equipment, contracts with synthetic biology labs, personnel requisitions for specialists in non-human neurogenetics. The trail leads to a facility in the Foundry district — a Meridian BioDiv laboratory designated only by a number, no name.
Lab 17. That's where Eclipse lives.

delf_042.title: Safe Harbor
text: Veya knows the surface better than you — decades of Shadow operations have given her a map of safe houses, friendly contacts, and hidden spaces throughout the Lower District. She leads you to a location you wouldn't have found on your own: an abandoned server farm in the sub-levels of the Transit Hub, three floors below public access.

The server racks are dead, their processing cores stripped for parts by scavengers years ago. But the infrastructure is sound: reinforced walls, climate control, multiple exits, and — crucially — a pre-installed electromagnetic shielding that blocks external surveillance. Someone, at some point, built this place to be invisible.
'I've been maintaining it as an emergency bolt-hole for two years,' Veya says, activating a hidden power cell that brings the lighting online. 'Never needed it. Until now.'

The space is larger than your maintenance corridor base — thirty by forty feet, with separate areas that could serve as sleeping quarters, a work station, and a meeting room. It's not the warrens. It's not home. But it's dark, it's secure, and it's yours.
Veya pulls a crate from behind the dead servers. Inside: equipment. A surveillance kit, a comm system, a small weapons cache. Shadow supplies, stashed against a rainy day that has now arrived.
'Welcome to House Vel'tharin-in-exile,' she says. 'Population: two.'

choices.text: Reach out to other dark elf exiles

delf_043.title: Inside Lab 17
text: Lab 17 is a fortress disguised as a warehouse. From outside: corrugated metal walls, loading docks, the unremarkable exterior of a hundred other industrial buildings in the Foundry district. Inside: biometric locks, armed guards, and a clean-room facility that houses the most ambitious bioengineering project Meridian has ever attempted.

You go in at night, wrapped in the First Shadow, moving through the ventilation system with the fluid silence of someone who's been infiltrating hostile spaces since before most of the guards were born. Dark elf physiology was made for this: slim build, flexible joints, night vision that turns the unlit vents into well-mapped corridors.
What you find in the lab's central chamber stops you cold.

Three tanks, six feet tall, filled with viscous amber liquid. Inside each tank, a figure floats in fetal position. Humanoid. Dark-skinned. Pointed ears. They're dark elves — or something that looks like dark elves. But wrong, somehow. Too uniform. Too perfect. The faces are identical, and the expression on each face is the blankness of a machine that hasn't been activated yet.
Synthetic dark elves. Three prototypes. Nearly complete.

A data terminal near the tanks shows the project timeline. Full activation in six weeks. Combat testing in eight. Field deployment in twelve. Meridian is weeks away from making real dark elves obsolete.

choices.text: Download the data and plan a larger strike
Find the genetic templates — destroy the source

delf_044.title: Patient Threads
text: The Weaver's way: gather before you strike. You spend three weeks mapping every aspect of Lab 17's operations. Guard rotations, delivery schedules, personnel shift changes, communication protocols. You tail the lab's lead researcher — a human geneticist named Dr. Kira Mendez — and map her daily routine with the precision of a predator learning its prey's habits.

Veya contributes her Shadow expertise, running surveillance on the facility from multiple angles. She plants micro-cameras in the building's exterior — devices so small they're indistinguishable from dust particles, each one transmitting to your server farm base.

The intelligence picture develops slowly, like a photograph in chemical solution. Lab 17 has three research teams working in isolation — none of them has full knowledge of the project. Classic compartmentalization. Team One handles genetic sequencing. Team Two handles neural architecture. Team Three handles the shadow-affinity replication — the most difficult and most secret component.

Team Three is the keystone. Without the shadow-affinity replication, the prototypes are just synthetic humanoids with dark elf features. Impressive but not threatening. The shadow arts are what make them dangerous. And Team Three is led by someone whose personnel file makes your blood run cold: a dark elf researcher named Veln, formerly of House Vel'tharin.
The Matriarch didn't just sell data. She sold people.

delf_045.title: The Scattered Houses
text: The dark elf diaspora on the surface is smaller than you expected. Most dark elves never leave the warrens — the underground is their world, and the surface is a foreign country. But exiles exist. The failed ambitious. The politically inconvenient. The ones who simply grew tired of centuries of shadow games and wanted to see the sun, even if it burned.

You find them in the Lower District's dark corners — working as information brokers, security consultants, nightclub owners. Each one carries the particular kind of damage that comes from being cut off from the warrens: the loneliness of living among people who can't see in the dark, who don't understand shadow protocol, who think that secrets are things you share.

A woman named Thessaly, formerly of House Syn'ari, runs a data brokerage from a bar in Sector 6. She was exiled thirty years ago for refusing to assassinate a child. She listens to your story about Project Eclipse with an expression that hardens by degrees.

'They're replacing us,' she says. 'The Houses sell our biology to the corporations, the corporations build synthetic copies, the copies make us obsolete, and the Houses die anyway. It's a perfect loop of self-destruction.'
'Unless we break the loop,' you say.
She studies you with eyes that have seen three decades of surface life. 'What do you need?'

choices.text: Build a coalition of exiles against Eclipse

delf_046.title: Sabotage
text: Three tanks. Three prototypes. Six weeks from activation. You don't have six weeks. You don't have six minutes — the lab's internal security will detect an unauthorized presence the moment you touch anything connected to the main systems.
But you have the Third Shadow. The forbidden art. The Deep Dark.

You draw it from the quantum gaps between photons, pulling darkness so absolute that it negates electromagnetic radiation. The Deep Dark sphere expands around the central tank — the primary prototype, the one closest to completion — and everything within the sphere ceases to exist on an electromagnetic level. The monitoring equipment goes silent. The life support alarms don't trigger because the sensors within the sphere have stopped functioning.

Inside the sphere, you work quickly. The tank's power coupling. The nutrient feed. The neural interface cables. You don't destroy them — destruction leaves evidence. You alter them. Subtle changes that will corrupt the prototype's development over the next three weeks, introducing cascading failures that will look like natural degradation.

When you withdraw the Deep Dark and leave through the ventilation system, the lab looks untouched. The monitoring equipment comes back online and reports nominal status. Dr. Mendez will continue her work without suspecting that the primary prototype is already failing.
By the time they realize what happened, you'll have had three weeks to plan the next move.

choices.text: Return to base and plan the next phase
[Dexterity 11] Vanish before they can trace the sabotage.

delf_047.title: The Data Heist
text: Information over action. The Weaver's instinct. You bypass the prototypes and head for the data terminal, drawing enough data to understand the full scope of Project Eclipse.

The download takes ninety seconds — an eternity in an infiltration, spent with your back exposed and the sound of guard footsteps echoing from the corridor. You fill a data crystal with everything: genetic sequences, neural architecture schematics, shadow-affinity replication protocols, project timelines, personnel files, and — most critically — the original data provided by the Matriarch.

The Matriarch's contribution is extensive. She didn't just sell genetic samples. She sold operational data. Shadow art training methodologies. Psychological conditioning protocols from the Crucible. The complete blueprint for creating a dark elf operative, from birth to deployment. Everything the House had learned in centuries of breeding and training warriors, compressed into a data package and sold for corporate credits.

You pocket the crystal and leave the way you came. The data is more valuable than any amount of sabotage. With this information, you can expose Eclipse publicly. You can prove that Meridian is creating synthetic soldiers using stolen biological data. And you can prove that the Matriarch of House Vel'tharin sold her own people for money.
The question is: who do you share it with?

choices.text: Return to base and analyze the data
[Tech 11] Encrypt the data. It's too valuable to lose.

delf_048.title: The Source
text: The genetic templates are stored in a cryogenic vault in the lab's sub-basement — the most secure area, protected by biometric locks and a dedicated guard. But you've been studying this facility for weeks, and you know something the guards don't: the vault's climate control system shares a ventilation shaft with the lab above. A shaft barely wide enough for a human. But you are not human, and dark elves were built for tight spaces.

You slide through the shaft like water through a crack, emerging in the vault's ceiling space. From above, you can see the storage units: twelve cryogenic cases, each containing a genetic template — the biological samples the Matriarch sold to Meridian. Dark elf DNA, preserved in crystal matrices, waiting to be replicated into an army of soulless copies.

You drop into the vault silently and consider your options. Taking the templates removes the raw material. Meridian can't create synthetic dark elves without the genetic source code. But carrying twelve cryogenic cases out of a secure facility is impractical. You can take two, maybe three.
Or you can destroy them. The Deep Dark, applied to biological material, doesn't just erase it — it decoheres the molecular structure. The templates would become dust. Irrecoverable.

You choose destruction. Some things cannot be allowed to exist in corporate hands. You draw the Deep Dark around each case, one by one, and feel the genetic legacy of your species dissolve into quantum noise.

choices.text: [Dexterity 10] Use the tunnel ways. The surface routes are compromised.

delf_049.title: The Defector
text: Veln. Formerly House Vel'tharin, Sigil-class researcher, specialist in shadow-art neurophysiology. He left the warrens voluntarily ten years ago — or was allowed to leave, which in the Matriarch's vocabulary amounts to the same thing. He was assigned to Meridian as part of the deal, a living component of the data package.

You find him in a noodle shop near the Foundry, eating alone, staring at the wall with the particular emptiness of someone who has forgotten how to enjoy food. He looks like a dark elf who's been on the surface too long — his eyes are permanently squinted against the light, his skin has lost the deep indigo luster that comes from a lifetime in properly dark environments.
You sit across from him and speak in the old tongue. 'The Matriarch sent you here to help them build copies of us. How does that feel?'
He flinches. He looks at you. He recognizes what you are — not specifically who, but what. Weaver. The Matriarch's instrument. He puts down his chopsticks with hands that tremble.

'They're close,' he says. 'Six weeks. The shadow-affinity replication is the last component. I've been... I've been stalling. Introducing delays. But I can't stall forever. They'll replace me or they'll find another source.'
'We're going to stop it,' you say. 'With or without you. But with you would be faster.'

choices.text: Recruit Veln to sabotage from inside
Get his intelligence and plan the Lab 17 strike

delf_050.title: The Exile Coalition
text: The coalition forms slowly — dark elf exiles are, by nature and training, suspicious of collective action. But the threat of Eclipse cuts through decades of inter-House rivalry and individual paranoia. Seven exiles from five different Houses answer your call, meeting in the server farm safe house with the wary posture of predators forced to share a water hole.

Thessaly from House Syn'ari. A former Blade named Wraith from House Dre'vak. A Sigil archivist named Sel from House Mol'thas. Two independent operators who never had House affiliation. And you and Veya, the last threads of House Vel'tharin.

You present the intelligence on Project Eclipse with the methodical precision of a Weaver's briefing. The room goes quiet. You can feel the collective fury — cold, controlled, channeled inward the way dark elves channel everything — building like pressure behind a dam.

'This is not a House problem,' Thessaly says. 'This is a species problem. If Meridian succeeds, every dark elf — in the warrens or on the surface — becomes redundant. Our biology, our arts, our identity — all replaceable by a product line.'
'So what do we do?' Wraith asks.

You look at the assembled exiles — broken, displaced, cut off from everything they knew — and you see something the Houses never could: a network that owes no loyalty to any Matriarch. A free intelligence operation. The first dark elf collective that isn't a House.
'We do what we've always done,' you say. 'We work in the dark. We pull threads. And we destroy what needs destroying.'

choices.text: [Charisma 11] Rally the coalition with words, not just evidence.

delf_051.title: Aftermath
text: Back at base, you assess the damage — to Eclipse, and to yourself. The Lab 17 operation was a success, but it was also a beginning. Meridian will investigate. They'll discover the sabotage, or the data theft, or the destroyed templates. And they'll come looking for whoever did it.

The hunt will be methodical. Meridian's security division isn't the blunt instrument of street-level enforcement — it's a sophisticated intelligence operation with resources that dwarf anything the Houses ever commanded. They'll analyze the infiltration pattern, identify the techniques used, and recognize them as dark elf in origin. From there, they'll narrow the suspect pool to exiles with the skill set to breach a secure facility.
The pool is small. Your face — your real face, not a Shadow-mask — will be on their list within weeks.

But you have something Meridian doesn't: the experience of being hunted by experts. The Crucible taught you to operate under surveillance. The warrens taught you to function in environments where every shadow might contain an enemy. The surface has its own challenges, but the fundamental skill — existing while being sought — is one you've practiced since childhood.
'We need to disappear for a while,' Veya says. 'Go deep. Let the trail cool.'
'Disappearing is what we do,' you reply.

choices.text: Establish a new identity in the Lower District
Go deeper underground — the old tunnels

delf_052.title: The New Face
text: Identity construction is a Shadow art, and Veya is a master. She builds you a surface persona from scratch: documentation, history, behavioral patterns. The persona is designed to be unremarkable — a dark elf immigrant working odd jobs in Sector 7, no criminal record, no corporate affiliations, no thread that anyone would think to pull.

The physical component is harder. Dark elves are distinctive — the deep indigo skin, the white or silver hair, the eyes that reflect light like a cat's. You can't change your biology, but you can change how people see it. Posture adjustments that make you seem shorter, less graceful, less dangerous. Clothing that conceals the predator's build beneath layers of shabby fabric. A slight hunch that eliminates the unconscious aristocratic bearing that the Crucible drilled into every graduate.

Within a week, you're invisible. Not Shadow-invisible — socially invisible. Just another displaced non-human in the Lower District, trying to survive in a city that treats its underclass as interchangeable. The anonymity chafes. You were raised to be exceptional. Being mediocre, even as a disguise, feels like a small death.

But it works. The patrol drones scan you and move on. The neighborhood vendors nod without interest. The information brokers you cultivated don't recognize you. You are no one. And no one is the safest thing to be when Meridian is hunting.

choices.text: [Charisma 10] Build the new identity carefully. Every detail matters.

delf_053.title: The Deep Dark Tunnels
text: Below the city's infrastructure, below the warrens, below everything built by hands, the natural cave systems extend into the earth's crust. You descend into passages that haven't been walked by any sentient being in centuries, navigating by touch and the deep-dark sense that dark elves carry in their nervous systems like a compass needle that always points down.

In the deepest chambers, the darkness is absolute. Not the managed darkness of the warrens, not the partial darkness of the surface night. Real darkness. The darkness that existed before light, the primordial absence that your people chose as their home millennia ago.

You sit in it and breathe. The Third Shadow responds to this environment like a plant responds to water — it fills you, flows through you, becomes indistinguishable from the darkness around you. For a while, you cease to be a physical being and become instead a pattern in the dark, a consciousness without a body, existing in the space between photons.

In that space, you think clearly for the first time since the exile. The rage, the grief, the disorientation of surface life — all of it fades. What remains is the essential you: the Weaver, the pattern-reader, the thread-puller. The Matriarch gave you these skills. The House gave you these skills. But the skills are yours. They belong to you regardless of who you serve or where you stand.
You are not House Vel'tharin's instrument. You are your own.

choices.text: Return to the surface with clarity
Carry the deep dark with you. It's part of who you are.

delf_054.title: The New Weave
text: Weeks pass. The surface world, which initially felt like an alien planet, begins to resolve into something navigable. You learn the rhythms of the Lower District — the morning rush of workers heading to the Foundry, the afternoon lull when the patrol drones thin out, the evening surge of activity in the Neon Strip, the deep-night quiet when the real business happens.

You build a new network. Not the House's network — something smaller, looser, built on different principles. The House operated through fear and obligation. Your network operates through mutual interest. You trade information with Thessaly's data brokerage. You provide security analysis for businesses in the Market District. You serve as a go-between for parties who can't meet publicly.

Veya works the other end — maintaining contacts from her Shadow days, running her own intelligence operations, feeding data back to the exile coalition. Together, you build something that isn't quite a House and isn't quite a business. It's a capability. A distributed intelligence network, invisible and flexible, with no throne and no Matriarch.

The Matriarch would be horrified. An intelligence network without hierarchy? Without a central authority? Without the ruthless discipline that kept House Vel'tharin operational for four centuries? Impossible. Unworkable.
And yet. It works. Because the one thing the Matriarch never understood is that people who choose to cooperate are more reliable than people who are compelled to obey.

delf_055.title: Meridian Moves
text: Eclipse doesn't die quietly. Meridian pours resources into recovery — new genetic samples sourced from other dark elf contacts, accelerated timelines, increased security around the surviving research. The sabotage bought time, but time is finite, and Meridian's patience is backed by functionally unlimited capital.

Your network picks up the signals: Meridian recruiters in the underground, offering dark elves money for blood samples. Corporate security teams sweeping the tunnel network near the warrens. A sudden increase in drone surveillance in Sectors 5 through 8, the areas with the highest dark elf population density.

They're adapting. Kael'ris's coup and your sabotage disrupted their original supply chain — the Matriarch's cooperation — so they're going direct. Cutting out the middleman and sourcing dark elf biology from the population itself. Voluntarily if possible. By force if necessary.

The exile coalition reports three incidents of dark elves being detained by unmarked security teams. None of the detained have resurfaced. The word on the underground network is short and terrifying: harvesting.
The clock is ticking. Eclipse is wounded, but it's not dead. And every day you wait is a day closer to Meridian's synthetic dark elves becoming reality.

choices.text: Go public — expose Eclipse to the world

delf_056.title: War Footing
text: You gather the exile coalition in the server farm safe house and lay out the tactical situation. Seven exiles, plus Veya, plus the contacts you've cultivated in the Lower District.
