Against: the largest corporation in the city, with military-grade security, unlimited funding, and the tacit support of a city government that treats corporate interests as law.
Wraith, the former Blade, cracks his knuckles. 'I've been waiting for this.'

'This isn't a straight fight,' you say. 'We can't match Meridian's firepower. We match their intelligence. We know things they don't know we know. We can move in spaces they don't know exist. We fight this as dark elves — from the shadows, with precision, targeting the specific vulnerabilities that make Eclipse possible.'
You identify three targets: the research personnel, the supply chain, and the political cover. Remove any one of them, and Eclipse stalls. Remove all three, and it dies.

Thessaly takes the political angle — she has contacts in the media and the district council. Wraith takes the supply chain — disruption and sabotage in the underground, cutting the flow of resources. You and Veya take the research personnel — not killing them, but compromising them. Turning them. Making them afraid to continue.
The Weaver's art, applied at scale. Not pulling one thread but unraveling an entire section of the weave.

delf_057.title: Into the Light
text: Going public is the most un-dark-elf strategy imaginable. Your people have spent millennia building power through secrecy. The idea of deliberately exposing information — making it free, making it visible, giving it away — violates every principle the Houses taught.

But the Houses were wrong. Secrecy protects the secret-keeper, but it also isolates them. If Eclipse succeeds, it succeeds in silence, and the dark elves die in silence, and nobody on the surface ever knows or cares. The only way to make the surface world care about the fate of a species it barely knows exists is to make them see it.

You leak the Eclipse data to three independent news feeds simultaneously, using sanitized channels that can't be traced back to your network. You include enough documentation to be credible: genetic sequences, project timelines, Meridian internal memos. You hold back the most sensitive data — the shadow art protocols, the Crucible methodology — because some secrets still need keeping.

The story breaks like a shadow-charge: silent, devastating, impossible to ignore. 'MERIDIAN BUILDING SYNTHETIC DARK ELF ARMY.' The headlines are sensational, as headlines tend to be. But the underlying truth is worse than the headlines: a corporation is attempting to commodify an entire species, turning sentient beings into product specifications.
For the first time in their history, dark elves are visible. And the surface world is watching.

choices.text: [Charisma 10] Spin the fallout into an advantage.

delf_058.title: The Underground Warning
text: Before anything else: protect your people. Even the people who exiled you. Even the House that cast you out. Because species loyalty runs deeper than House politics, and the dark elves — all of them, warren-born and surface-living — deserve to know that Meridian is hunting them.

You compose a message in the old dark tongue, encrypted with a cipher that only dark-elf-trained Sigils can break. The message is distributed through every underground network you can reach — dead drops, data brokers, tunnel runners, bartenders in dark elf-frequented establishments.

The message is simple: 'Meridian is harvesting dark elf biology for Project Eclipse — synthetic reproduction of our species. Blood samples and genetic material are being collected by force. Protect yourselves. Trust no one who offers payment for biological data. Move in groups. Stay in secured areas. This is not a drill.'

Within days, the dark elf underground transforms. The casual, fractured community of exiles and loners becomes a tight, paranoid, mutually protective network. Dark elves who haven't spoken to each other in decades start sharing safe house locations. Information flows through channels that Meridian can't tap because they're conducted in a language Meridian can't read.
The warrens hear the message too. You know because the Matriarch's response arrives via a dead drop you'd forgotten existed: a single word in the family cipher. 'Good.'

choices.text: [Wisdom 11] Anticipate Meridian's response before it comes.

delf_059.title: The Response
text: Meridian's response is immediate and overwhelming. Corporate security sweeps the Lower District with a force level normally reserved for riot suppression. Drone density doubles. Checkpoints appear at transit hub entrances. Every dark elf in the district is stopped, scanned, and logged.

They're looking for you. The infiltration pattern from Lab 17 has been analyzed, and someone in Meridian's security division has correctly identified it as the work of a warren-trained operative. They don't know your name — the Matriarch erased it, and ironically that erasure now protects you — but they know what you are. And they're flooding the district with enough surveillance to make shadow-cloaking almost impossible.

Almost. Not quite. The First Shadow still works in the deepest maintenance corridors, where the drone coverage is thinnest. And the Third Shadow — the Deep Dark — creates zones of absolute sensory negation that no surveillance technology can penetrate.
But you can't live in the Deep Dark permanently. You need to move, to eat, to interact with the surface world that has become your home. And every time you drop the shadow, you're visible.

The exile coalition scatters, going to ground in safe houses across the district. Veya vanishes into a deep-cover persona so thorough that even you can't find her. The network holds, but it's stretched thin.

choices.text: Adapt and survive — go to ground
Fight back — strike while they're focused on you

delf_060.title: Going to Ground
text: You go dark. Fully dark. Abandon the surface, descend into the deep tunnels, and wait. The Weaver's patience — the ability to sit in silence for days, observing, analyzing, letting the weave's pattern reveal itself without interference.

In the deep tunnels, Meridian can't reach you. Their technology is designed for surface environments — controlled atmospheres, electrical infrastructure, electromagnetic communication. Down here, in the raw stone and the primordial dark, their advantages evaporate.

You spend two weeks in the deep. Eating from the survival cache you stashed during your initial surface exploration. Sleeping in the warm pockets near geothermal vents. Practicing the shadow arts in an environment so perfectly dark that the boundaries between your body and the darkness become theoretical.

And you think. You Weave. You take every thread you've gathered — Eclipse, the Matriarch, the exile coalition, Meridian's response, the dark elf diaspora, the city's power structure — and you weave them together in your mind until the pattern emerges.

When you surface, two weeks later, you know what to do. Not the next step. The whole path. The complete weave, from this moment to the endgame. It will take years. It will require every skill the Crucible taught you and several it didn't. But the pattern is clear.
You step into the rain and begin.

choices.text: The path forward is through information, not force.

delf_061.title: Counter-Strike
text: Meridian is flooding the district with security. They're looking outward, scanning for threats in the population. Which means they're not looking inward, at their own infrastructure. The Weaver sees the gap.

You don't target the security forces — that's a fight you can't win. You target the logistics. The communication relays that coordinate the drone sweeps. The fuel depots that power the patrol vehicles. The data uplinks that connect the street-level scanners to Meridian's central processing.

Surgical strikes, executed alone, using the shadow arts and the Deep Dark to create windows of absolute invisibility. You destroy a comm relay in the Foundry district. You corrupt a data uplink in the Transit Hub. You sabotage a fuel depot in the Scrap Yards. Each attack is small, precise, and untraceable.

The effect is cumulative. Meridian's security sweep stutters, its coordination degraded by the loss of infrastructure it took for granted. The drone coverage develops gaps. The checkpoints become sporadic. The overwhelming presence becomes merely intimidating.

It's not a victory. But it's proof that one dark elf with the right skills can make a corporation feel pain. And pain is information. It tells Meridian that hunting dark elves has a cost, and costs compound.

choices.text: Regroup and plan the next phase
Strike again while they're reeling. Patience is overrated.

delf_062.title: The Long Thread
text: The dust settles. Meridian's security presence eases — not withdrawing, but normalizing, becoming part of the district's background radiation rather than an acute threat. Eclipse continues in some form — you've delayed it, damaged it, but not killed it. Corporations don't die from single wounds. They adapt, reroute, find new paths to the same objectives.

But so do you. The exile coalition reforms, stronger than before. The crisis forged bonds that peacetime never could. Dark elves who spent decades avoiding each other now share intelligence, resources, and the guarded trust of people who've stood on the same side of a fight.

Veya resurfaces with new intelligence: Meridian has shifted Eclipse to a new facility, location unknown. The project is alive, but it's hurt. The timeline has been pushed back months, maybe years. And the public exposure — however briefly it lasted before Meridian's PR machine buried it — has planted a seed of doubt in the city's consciousness.

You sit in your maintenance corridor base in Sector 7, surrounded by data crystals and surveillance readouts, and you see the weave stretching out before you. Not months. Years. Decades. The fight against Eclipse will be long. The fight for the dark elves' right to exist as they are — not as corporate products — will be longer still.
But you are a dark elf. And dark elves measure time in centuries.

choices.text: [Wisdom 10] See the patterns. Choose the right thread to pull.

delf_063.title: The Crossroads
text: A message arrives on your datapad. No sender. A location pin: Mako's Repair Shop, three blocks east. And beneath the pin, a line of text that makes the Weaver in you sit up and pay attention: 'I have information about Eclipse's new location. And about the Matriarch.'

The threads converge. Your two battles — the fight against Meridian's Eclipse project and the unresolved reckoning with the House that forged and discarded you — are about to intersect. Whoever sent this message knows enough about both to bait the hook with exactly the right lure.
Veya's voice on the comm: 'I got the same message. Different dead drop. Same content.'
Someone is pulling your threads. A Weaver's nightmare and a Weaver's dream: someone who sees the pattern and wants to change it.

You check your equipment. Obsidian blade. Shadow arts. The skills of a lifetime spent in darkness. The hard-won knowledge of a surface world you've learned to navigate. And the cold, clear purpose that exile has refined into something sharper than any blade the Crucible ever produced.
Time to see who's at the other end of the thread.

choices.text: Face the future as an Assassin — the Blade's edge in the dark
Face the future as a Spymaster — the Weaver of a new network
Face the future as a Rogue Scholar — keeper of forbidden knowledge
Face the future as a Shadowbroker — power through secrets

delf_064.title: The Matriarch's Shadow
text: In quiet moments — the small hours between operations, when the surface world sleeps and the darkness of your maintenance corridor feels almost like home — you think about the Matriarch. Not with hate. Not with love. With the complex, layered understanding that only a Weaver can hold: the ability to see someone's pattern in its entirety, including the threads you wish didn't exist.

Vel'thara Syn ruled for four centuries because she understood something fundamental about power: it doesn't come from strength or intelligence or even information. It comes from pattern recognition. The ability to see where every thread is going before it arrives, and to position yourself at the convergence point.

She taught you that skill. And then she used it to discard you, because you saw a thread she didn't want seen. The irony is perfect. The instrument she forged was too sharp — it cut through her own concealment.

You don't forgive her. Forgiveness implies that what she did was personal. It wasn't. It was architectural. She made a structural decision about the House, and you were a structural element that didn't fit the new design. In her position, with her information, the decision was logical.
That's the worst part. The decision was logical. And logic, without mercy, is just cruelty with better math.

choices.text: Let the past inform, not control
The Matriarch's shadow is long. But you cast your own now.

delf_065.title: Surface Sunrise
text: You see your first sunrise by accident. An operation runs long, and you're caught above ground when the dawn breaks through a gap in the cloud cover. The light hits the city's upper levels and cascades down through the stacked architecture in shafts of gold and amber, reaching the Lower District as a diffused, warm glow that transforms the grimy streets into something almost beautiful.

You flinch. Every instinct screams: shadow. Conceal. Darkness is safety. But you force yourself to stand still, to let the light touch your skin, to experience the phenomenon that surface-dwellers take for granted and dark elves regard with ancestral suspicion.

It hurts. Not physically — your eyes adjusted to surface light weeks ago. It hurts emotionally. The sunrise is beautiful, and beauty on this scale is something the warrens never offered. The warrens offered security, power, sophistication — but never this raw, overwhelming, uncalculated beauty. A star burning millions of miles away, its light filtered through atmosphere and architecture, arriving at your upturned face as warmth and color. No dark elf designed this. No Matriarch controls it. It simply is.

You stand in the light for three minutes. Then you draw the First Shadow and disappear, because old habits are older than understanding. But the memory of the sunrise stays with you — a thread in the weave that you didn't expect, connecting you to the surface world in a way that no intelligence operation ever could.

choices.text: The sun is still alien. But you're learning its language.

delf_066.title: Kael'ris Returns
text: Three months after the coup, Kael'ris surfaces. He walks into a bar in Sector 7 where you've been maintaining a low-key surveillance post, sits down across from you, and orders a drink he doesn't know the name of. He's thinner than you remember, his face carved by months of hiding in the deep tunnels, his Blade's composure frayed at the edges by an experience he was never trained for: being alone.
'The deep tunnels are terrible,' he says conversationally, as though commenting on the weather. 'No structure. No chain of command. No one to kill. I nearly went insane from the boredom.'
'You came up.'

'I came up.' He drinks. Grimaces. Looks at the glass with the bewildered expression of someone encountering alcohol that hasn't been distilled in the warrens' centuries-old tradition. 'I heard about Eclipse. About what you did to Lab 17. Good work.'

The compliment — so rare between you that it's almost a foreign language — lands with unexpected weight. Kael'ris is a Blade. Blades don't admire Weaver work. They consider it unnecessarily complicated. For him to acknowledge your operation is an admission that the world has changed, and the old categories no longer apply.
'I want in,' he says. 'Whatever you're building. I want in.'

choices.text: Welcome him — the network needs a Blade
[Wisdom 10] Test his loyalty before trusting him fully.

delf_067.title: The Network Grows
text: The exile coalition evolves into something unprecedented in dark elf history: a voluntary organization. No Matriarch. No throne. No bloodline hierarchy. Members join because they choose to, contribute what they can, and leave if they want. It's messy, inefficient, and occasionally chaotic. It's also the most resilient intelligence network you've ever built.

Thessaly handles data brokerage — buying and selling information on the open market, generating the revenue that keeps the network operational. Wraith runs security — physical protection for network members, counter-surveillance, and the occasional direct-action operation against Meridian assets. Ven, the young Sigil, builds the technical infrastructure — encrypted communications, data analysis tools, the digital backbone that connects the network's nodes.

And you weave. You sit at the center of the network — not as a leader, but as a coordinator, reading the patterns in the information that flows through the system, identifying threats and opportunities, pulling threads when necessary. The Matriarch would call it a failure of leadership. You call it a feature.

A dark elf collective without a throne. A shadow network that operates by consensus. It shouldn't work. But it does. And every day it survives is a refutation of everything the Houses taught about power.

choices.text: The network is your weave now. Tend it carefully.

delf_068.title: The Shadow Market
text: You discover, almost by accident, that the surface world has its own shadow market — an informal economy of secrets, favors, and back-channel negotiations that operates beneath the official commercial structure. It's cruder than the House system, less elegant, but it runs on the same fuel: information.

You integrate yourself into this economy with the ease of a fish entering water. Dark elf information-trading skills, honed over millennia of inter-House competition, are overkill in the surface market. You can read a negotiation partner's true position in the first thirty seconds of a meeting. You can identify the leverage points in a business deal before the contract is drafted. You can predict market movements by analyzing the body language of the people making the decisions.

Within weeks, you have a reputation as someone who knows things. Not specific things — you're careful about what you reveal — but someone who understands the shape of the game. People come to you for advice. For analysis. For the Weaver's special talent: seeing patterns in what others perceive as chaos.

You charge for your services. Not much — enough to survive, enough to fund the network. The real payment is the information itself, the ever-expanding picture of the city's power structure that accumulates in your mental files with every interaction.

choices.text: [Intelligence 10] Map the shadow market's power structure.

delf_069.title: Teaching the Shadow
text: Ven, the young Sigil, asks you to teach him the shadow arts. In the warrens, this would be protocol — knowledge flows through authorized channels, from master to student, in a controlled environment. On the surface, it's an act of rebellion. The shadow arts are House knowledge. Sharing them outside the House structure is forbidden.
But the Houses didn't share. The Houses hoarded. And hoarding is how species go extinct.

You teach Ven the First Shadow in a deep-tunnel chamber, correcting his form with the precise, patient methodology of someone who was taught by the best. His concealment field is shaky at first — the light bends improperly, creating a visible shimmer that would get him killed in the warrens. But he's talented, and he's determined, and after a week of practice his First Shadow is functional.

More importantly, you teach him why. Not just the technique, but the philosophy. The shadow arts are not about hiding. They're about understanding the nature of perception — what is seen, what is hidden, and the vast space between them where reality is negotiable. A dark elf who understands this can navigate any environment, surface or subterranean, because the fundamental principles are the same.
'The warrens are gone,' you tell Ven. 'But the shadow remains. It goes wherever we go. It belongs to us, not to the Houses.'

choices.text: The shadow is patient. So are you.

delf_070.title: Veya's Cost
text: Veya asks to meet at the Glass Eye. When you arrive, she's sitting in her usual booth, but the Shadow-mask is gone. Her real face — the one you grew up with, the one that smiled with calculated warmth in the warrens and genuine warmth in the rare moments she let her guard down — is drawn and tired.

'I can't hold the covers anymore,' she says. 'The personas. The masks. I've been wearing other people's faces for so long that I can't find mine. I look in the mirror and I see assets. Identities I've built and discarded. I count seven faces and I don't know which one is real.'

Shadow dissolution. You've heard of it — it's an occupational hazard for long-term Shadow operatives. The sustained maintenance of false identities erodes the authentic self until the operative can no longer distinguish between performance and reality. In the warrens, it was managed through regular debriefing and identity-reinforcement sessions with Sigil psychologists. On the surface, there are no Sigils. There is no support system. There is just Veya, alone with a gallery of faces and no map back to her own.

You take her hand. 'Your name is Veya. You are my sister. You taught me to cheat at shadow-chess when we were twelve. You once put a live scorpion in Kael'ris's boot and laughed for three days. You are not an asset. You are a person. And you are real.'
She holds your hand so tight that her nails leave marks. But she smiles. And the smile is hers.

choices.text: Veya's cost is your burden. Carry it.

delf_071.title: The Warren's Echo
text: You dream of the warrens. Not the political intrigues, not the Crucible, not the Matriarch's obsidian eyes. You dream of the physical space — the carved corridors, the shadow-lit halls, the deep quiet of stone that has been home to dark elves for centuries. The sound of water dripping in the cistern. The smell of obsidian dust. The texture of a wall that's been polished smooth by generations of passing hands.

The warrens were not kind. But they were complete. A world designed for your species, by your species, to the specifications of your biology. Every surface, every light level, every acoustic property was calibrated for dark elf comfort. Moving through the warrens was like swimming in water designed for your body — effortless, natural, correct.

The surface will never be that. No matter how long you live here, no matter how well you adapt, the surface world will always be a foreign country. The light will always be too bright. The noise will always be too loud. The spaces will always be too open. You are a creature of the deep, and the deep is closed to you.

But the tunnels beneath the city are not the warrens, and they are not closed. And in their raw, natural darkness, you can sometimes — for a few hours, in the deepest chambers — feel something like home.

choices.text: [Dexterity 10] Move through the warren's echo without disturbing it.

delf_072.title: The Blade's Edge
text: A Meridian extraction team targets a dark elf family in Sector 5. Two parents, one child. The parents are geneticists who left the warrens voluntarily twenty years ago. The child was born on the surface. They have nothing to do with the Houses, nothing to do with Eclipse, nothing to do with the shadow games that shape the dark elf world. They're just people.

You intercept the extraction team in a maintenance corridor. Four operatives, armed, armored, carrying the chemical restraints that Meridian uses for biological asset acquisition. They don't expect resistance — they've done this before, to other dark elves, without consequence.
The First Shadow drops. You appear in front of them like a geometric proof — dark, angular, inevitable. The obsidian blade catches the corridor light.

Three minutes. All four down. Non-lethal — you're not a Blade, whatever the Crucible tried to make you, and you don't kill people who are just following orders. But they won't be following orders for a while. Broken arms, dislocated shoulders, crushed hand bones. The precise, anatomical damage that says: the next time, it will be worse.

The family is safe. The child stares at you with dark violet eyes — dark elf eyes — and says, 'Are you a superhero?' You don't know how to answer. In the warrens, there were no heroes. Just threads and the people who pulled them.

choices.text: The blade cuts both ways. Remember that.

delf_073.title: Thessaly's Gift
text: Thessaly finds you at the Sector 7 bar on a night when the rain is heavy and the patrols are light. She slides a small package across the table — wrapped in dark cloth, heavy for its size.
'Open it when you're alone,' she says. 'It's from all of us. The coalition.'

You open it in your maintenance corridor. Inside: a new obsidian blade, forged in the traditional dark elf style but with modifications. The handle is wrapped in a synthetic material that provides better grip than traditional leather. The edge is honed to a molecular fineness that your original blade could never achieve. And etched into the flat of the blade, in the old dark script, is a single word: 'Kin.'

Not House. Not bloodline. Kin. The word dark elves use for chosen family — people bound not by birth but by shared purpose. A concept that the Houses discouraged, because chosen loyalty is harder to control than obligated loyalty.

You hold the blade and feel something that the warrens never taught you how to name. It takes a moment before you recognize it, because the Crucible trained it out of you with decades of careful conditioning.
Belonging. The blade says: you belong to us. Not as an instrument. Not as a Weaver. As kin.

choices.text: Carry the blade into the future
Accept Thessaly's gift. Allies are rare.

delf_074.title: The Architect's Eye
text: You begin to see the city differently. Not as a hostile environment to survive — the initial perception that dominated your first weeks on the surface — but as a system. A vast, interconnected, endlessly complex system that operates on principles remarkably similar to the warrens, just at a larger scale.

The warrens had Houses. The city has corporations. The warrens had shadow politics. The city has lobbying and media manipulation. The warrens had the Crucible. The city has the economic ladder, equally merciless in its selection of winners and losers.

The key difference: scale. The warrens housed thousands. The city houses millions. The weave is proportionally larger, and the patterns proportionally more complex. But complexity is not chaos. Complexity is just pattern at a resolution your eyes haven't adjusted to yet.

You spend a week on a rooftop in the Market District, shadow-cloaked, watching the city breathe. By the end of the week, you can see it: the macro-pattern, the fundamental weave that holds the city together. Power flows upward. Information flows downward — filtered, controlled, shaped. Money circulates in loops that always return more to the top than they distribute to the bottom.
It's not justice. But it is a pattern. And patterns, once understood, can be changed.

choices.text: [Intelligence 10] See the architecture of power. Find the weak points.

delf_075.title: The Last Message
text: A message arrives through the old family cipher. Veya decrypts it. The source is unmistakable: the Matriarch.

'Project Eclipse has moved to the orbital platform. Beyond your reach. The prototypes will be activated within the year. The Houses have agreed to provide genetic material in exchange for security contracts. The dark elf species, as an independent entity, has approximately ten years before synthetic replacements make us redundant. I tell you this because you deserve to know. And because I have concluded, after four centuries of certainty, that I may have been wrong. About the House. About the deal. About you. Do what I could not: find a way to save us that doesn't require selling what we are. — V.S.'

You read it twice. The Matriarch, admitting error. Four hundred years of iron certainty, cracked by the evidence of her own decisions' consequences. It's not an apology — she's incapable of apology. But it's an acknowledgment. And it's intelligence. The orbital platform. The acceleration of the timeline. The Houses' complicity.

The game has changed. Eclipse is beyond your physical reach. But information — the fundamental currency of the dark elf world — has no altitude limit. The fight continues. The weave extends. And somewhere, in the spaces between the threads, there is a path to saving your people.
You just have to see it.

choices.text: The last message is a warning. Heed it.

delf_076.title: A Quiet Moment
text: Rain on the window of your maintenance corridor. Through a gap in the wall — a crack you've deliberately left unrepaired because it lets in air and a narrow slice of the outside world — you can see the wet street below. People moving. Lives happening. The vast, messy, uncontrolled performance of a surface world that has no Matriarch, no Crucible, no shadow protocol.

You sit in your small dark space and you eat something warm from a street vendor's cart — a noodle soup that is objectively terrible by any culinary standard, but that tastes, tonight, like the first good thing you've eaten in months. The warmth settles in your stomach and radiates outward, and you recognize the sensation as comfort. Simple, animal comfort. A warm meal in a dry space while the rain falls outside.

The warrens never offered this. The warrens offered power, knowledge, purpose — but never comfort. Comfort was weakness. Comfort was an attachment that could be leveraged against you. In the Crucible, they taught you to endure discomfort so thoroughly that you forgot comfort existed.
You finish the soup and set the bowl aside. Outside, the rain continues. Inside, you are warm, and fed, and for this one moment, not fighting anything. Not weaving. Not analyzing. Just existing.
Tomorrow, the fight resumes. But tonight, you are allowed to be comfortable. And the permission comes from the only authority that matters now: yourself.

choices.text: Sleep and dream of new patterns
Quiet moments are rare. Don't waste this one.

delf_077.title: The Obsidian Mirror
text: You find an obsidian shard in the Scrap Yards — real obsidian, the volcanic glass that the warrens were carved from. It's the size of your palm, smooth on one side, rough on the other. You take it home and polish the smooth side until it's a mirror.

Dark elves don't use reflective mirrors. In the warrens, surfaces absorb light rather than reflect it. Your first encounter with your own reflection in surface mirrors was disorienting — the face that looked back was a stranger, all sharp angles and deep indigo skin and eyes that glowed faintly in the dark. You'd spent eighty years knowing your face only through the reactions of others.

Now, in the obsidian mirror, you see yourself in a medium that feels right. The volcanic glass shows your features in dark tones, shadow on shadow, the way dark elves are meant to be seen. And you see something you didn't expect: you look stronger. Not physically — thinner, actually, leaner from weeks of surface survival. But stronger in the eyes. The Crucible gave you discipline. The exile gave you something harder: purpose you chose yourself.
The face in the obsidian mirror is not the Matriarch's Weaver. It's not the House's instrument. It's yours. Just yours. And it is enough.

choices.text: [Wisdom 10] The obsidian mirror shows truth. Face it.

delf_078.title: The Crucible's Legacy
text: In the deep tunnels, in the dark, you run the Crucible patterns. Not the full program — that requires instructors, equipment, the infrastructure of the warrens. But the core movements: shadow-step, blade-work, concealment drill, perception exercise. The physical vocabulary of dark elf combat and espionage, practiced alone in a natural cave with no audience and no score.

You've been doing this every day since the exile. Not out of duty or discipline — those motivations belong to the House, and the House is no longer yours. You practice because the movements are beautiful. The shadow-step, executed properly, is a dance with photons — a conversation between your body and the light, conducted at the speed of reflex. The blade-work is geometry made physical, each cut and parry a theorem proven in steel.

The Crucible tried to make these movements into instruments of control. But the movements existed before the Crucible, before the Houses, before dark elf society organized itself into competing power structures. They emerged from the original relationship between dark elves and the darkness — a symbiosis so old that it's encoded in your DNA.

You practice in the dark, and the dark responds, and for an hour each day you are exactly what evolution intended: a being perfectly adapted to its environment, moving through shadow like thought through consciousness.

choices.text: The crucible forged you. Now forge your own path.

delf_079.title: The Price of Seeing
text: The Weaver's eye never turns off. You see patterns everywhere — in the movement of crowds, in the placement of security cameras, in the way a bartender pours drinks and which customer gets the fuller glass. Every interaction is data. Every relationship is a thread. Every day is a tapestry of interconnected events that your trained mind cannot stop analyzing.

It's exhausting. In the warrens, the Weaver's eye served a purpose — it informed the Matriarch's decisions, shaped the House's strategy. On the surface, without a hierarchy to serve, the eye just runs, cataloging everything, identifying threats that don't materialize, predicting patterns that lead nowhere. Your mind is a surveillance system with no one watching the monitors.
Veya notices. 'You're vibrating,' she says. 'You need to learn how to not see. How to let information flow past without catching it. Surface people do it every day — they call it relaxation.'
'Relaxation,' you repeat. The word tastes foreign.
'It's a skill. Like shadow-cloaking, but for your mind. You'll figure it out.' She pauses. 'Or you'll burn out. One or the other.'

You don't burn out. You learn, slowly, to modulate the Weaver's eye — to dial it down from full analysis to background monitoring, to let the world be messy and unread when the situation doesn't demand precision. It's the hardest thing you've learned on the surface. And possibly the most important.

choices.text: Find balance between seeing and living
[Intelligence 10] Seeing has a price. Calculate whether it's worth paying.

delf_080.title: Mira's Loyalty
text: Mira, the Blade who followed Kael'ris into exile, approaches you after a coalition meeting with a request. 'I want to learn the Weaving,' she says. 'Not the full art. Just enough to understand how you see. I'm tired of being a blade pointed at targets someone else chooses. I want to see the targets myself.'

A Blade asking to learn a Weaver's skills. In the warrens, the request would be denied — compartmentalization was absolute, each path separate, each skill set contained within its designated class. The idea of a Blade who could see the strategic picture was, to the Matriarch, as dangerous as a weapon that aimed itself.
But you are not the Matriarch. And the exile coalition is not a House.

You teach Mira the fundamentals of pattern recognition. Not the full Weaving — that takes decades — but the basic perceptual shifts that allow a trained observer to see connections between seemingly unrelated events. She takes to it with the focused intensity of a Blade learning a new combat form, and within weeks she's identifying supply chain patterns that your network can exploit.
A Blade who sees. A Weaver who fights. The old categories dissolving under the pressure of a new world. The Matriarch would say you're destroying tradition. You'd say you're evolving it.

choices.text: Continue building the new dark elf way
Mira's loyalty is earned, not given. Honor it.

delf_081.title: The Information Web
text: Your information network grows beyond the dark elf community. Surface-dwellers bring you intelligence in exchange for analysis, and the web of contacts extends into territories you never anticipated: an orc mechanic who hears things in the Foundry, a human journalist who tracks corporate activities, a synth bartender whose clients talk too freely after their third drink.

Each connection is a node. Each node generates data. And the data flows through your network like shadow through the warrens, accumulating in patterns that no single observer could identify but that your Weaver's eye reads like text.

You are building, you realize, what the Matriarch built. Not the dark elf version — the universal version. An intelligence network that transcends species, that operates on shared interest rather than shared blood, that values information for its own sake rather than as a weapon of intra-House competition.

The Matriarch's network served one House. Your network serves no one. It serves the idea that information should flow, that patterns should be seen, that the hidden architecture of power should be visible to anyone with the eyes to read it.
It's an idealistic vision for someone trained in the most cynical tradition on earth. But exile, you're learning, has a way of grinding cynicism into something sharper and more honest.

choices.text: [Tech 10] Use the information web. Every thread is a weapon.

delf_082.title: Night Operations
text: You run operations at night, in the dark, where your advantages are absolute. Surveillance runs through the Foundry district, mapping Meridian's evolving security infrastructure. Counter-intelligence sweeps to identify and neutralize tracking devices planted in the dark elf community. Data extraction from corporate systems, using access points that only someone with your shadow arts can reach.

The work is familiar. The loneliness isn't. In the warrens, every operation was part of a larger structure — the Matriarch set the objectives, the handlers provided support, the post-operation debriefs confirmed your value. On the surface, you set your own objectives, provide your own support, and debrief to an empty room.

Freedom, you're discovering, is heavier than obedience. Obedience only requires compliance. Freedom requires judgment. And judgment, exercised without a safety net, is the most exhausting form of labor that exists.

But the work gets done. Night after night, operation after operation, the intelligence picture grows clearer. Eclipse's supply chain mapped. Meridian's dark elf tracking database identified and partially corrupted. Three more extraction teams identified and their operational patterns cataloged for future interception.
You are one dark elf, working alone in the dark, against a corporation that spans a city. And you are winning. Slowly, invisibly, one thread at a time.

choices.text: [Dexterity 10] Move through the night like you were born in it. You were.

delf_083.title: The Surface Child
text: You encounter a dark elf child in the Market District. Six years old, born on the surface, raised in the light. She's never seen the warrens, never learned the old tongue, never practiced the shadow arts. She's playing with human children in a concrete playground, laughing, shrieking, her dark indigo skin flashing between the gray structures as she runs.

She doesn't know what she is. Her parents — surface-born themselves, third-generation exiles — have raised her as a city kid. No House training. No Crucible. No shadow protocol. She's growing up as just another Lower District child, with no more knowledge of her dark elf heritage than a half-remembered bedtime story about elves who lived underground.

You watch from the shadows and feel something complicated. Grief, perhaps, for the traditions that are dying. Relief, perhaps, that she'll never endure the Crucible. And underneath both: a question. Is it better to preserve a heritage that includes centuries of political assassination and psychological conditioning? Or is it better to let the surface wash it clean and start fresh?

You don't know the answer. The Weaver sees all threads, but some patterns resolve themselves only in time. The child will grow. She will discover her shadow-affinity, or she won't. She will find the old ways, or she won't. The thread is hers to weave.
You leave a small obsidian bead on the playground bench. If she finds it, she finds it. If she doesn't, she doesn't.

choices.text: Move on — the future writes itself
The surface child knows nothing of your world. Teach them.

delf_084.title: Wraith's Warning
text: Wraith corners you in the Sector 7 bar with intelligence that makes the hair on your neck stand up. 'The Matriarch isn't just selling to Meridian anymore. She's selling to anyone. Three corporations have active contracts for dark elf biological data. And she's begun selling operational intelligence — not just genetics. Safe house locations. Shadow network protocols. She's compromising our security to fund her escape plan.'

The Matriarch is dismantling the dark elf world from the inside, selling piece by piece to the highest bidder. Not out of malice — out of the same cold calculation that drove every decision she's ever made. If the House is dying, she'll monetize the corpse.
'We need to cut her off,' Wraith says. 'Not the House — the Matriarch personally. She's the single point of failure in this entire system. Remove her access to the data, and the sales stop.'
He's talking about assassination. The dark elf solution. Remove the problem by removing the person. Clean, efficient, final.
You shake your head. 'Killing her makes us the next generation of the same cycle. Matriarch rises. Matriarch falls. New Matriarch rises. The system perpetuates itself.'
'Then what?'
'We make her irrelevant. We build a dark elf world that doesn't need a Matriarch. That's the real revolution — not who sits on the throne, but whether the throne exists at all.'

choices.text: [Wisdom 11] Take Wraith's warning seriously. He sees things you don't.

delf_085.title: The Weave Holds
text: Months pass. The exile coalition stabilizes. Eclipse stalls. The dark elf community on the surface grows a little stronger, a little more connected, a little more aware of the threats it faces and the resources it commands. You have not won. But you have not lost. And for a species that measures time in centuries, not losing is its own kind of victory.

You sit in your maintenance corridor, reviewing the day's intelligence, and you see the weave. Not just the tactical picture — the larger pattern. Dark elves, displaced from their warrens, building a new existence on the surface. Not replacing what they lost but creating something different. A dark elf culture that isn't defined by Houses and Matriarchs but by chosen bonds and shared purpose.

It will take generations. The old ways are deep in the blood and the bone, and traditions don't die because someone decides they should. But traditions can evolve. And evolution, in the long view that dark elves are uniquely equipped to take, is just another word for survival.
Your datapad buzzes. The message from Mako's. The crossroads.

You stand, check your blade, draw the First Shadow to a comfortable half-cloak, and step into the corridor. Whatever waits at Mako's, you'll face it as what you've become: not a Weaver without a House, but a Weaver without a leash.

choices.text: The weave holds because you hold it. Don't let go.

delf_086.title: The Deep Dark Dream
text: In the deepest meditation, in the space between the Third Shadow and consciousness, you touch something that the Sigil scholars wrote about but no one in living memory has experienced: the Void Echo. The theoretical boundary between the Deep Dark and whatever lies beyond it.

The Void Echo is not darkness. Darkness is an absence of light. The Void Echo is an absence of absence — a space so fundamentally empty that even the concept of emptiness fails to describe it. Your consciousness brushes against it and recoils, not from pain but from the sheer incomprehensibility of what it encounters.

But in the recoil, you catch a flash of something. Not vision — dark elves don't process information as vision. A pattern. A vast, impossibly complex pattern, threading through the space beneath reality like roots through soil. A network. A web. Something alive and ancient and patient, operating on a scale that makes the city above seem like a dust mote on the surface of an ocean.

The Whisper Web? No — that's wood elf territory. This is something else. Something that belongs to the dark, the way the Whisper Web belongs to the green. An intelligence in the deep places that has been waiting, for millennia, for someone to notice it.
You pull back from the meditation, shaking, and file the experience under 'things to investigate when the world isn't on fire.' The list is getting long.

choices.text: Return to the surface with questions unanswered
The deep dark dreams are memories, not prophecy. Move forward.

delf_087.title: Rain and Reflection
text: You stand on a rooftop in Sector 7, letting the acid rain hit your upturned face. Your dark elf skin is more resistant to the chemical content than human skin — one of the biological adaptations that Meridian is so keen to replicate. The rain burns faintly, a tingling sensation that you've come to associate with the surface world's version of honesty: even the weather here is corrosive.

From this height, the Lower District unfolds beneath you like a circuit board — lights and lines and nodes of activity, each one a story, each story a thread in the incomprehensibly vast weave of the city. You can read some of the threads from here. The patrol routes. The commercial patterns. The slow, arterial flow of people moving through streets designed to keep them moving.

You've been on the surface for months now. Long enough to adapt. Long enough to build something. Long enough to learn that the surface world, for all its chaos and noise and unbearable light, has something the warrens never had: space. Space to be wrong. Space to fail. Space to try again. In the warrens, failure was permanent. Here, failure is just information.

The rain falls. You fall with it, descending from the rooftop with the fluid grace that no surface-dweller can match. Shadow-step, wall-run, silent landing. The city doesn't notice. It never does. That's fine. You don't need it to notice. You just need it to not burn.

choices.text: [Wisdom 10] Let the rain wash away the tunnel dust. Reflect.

delf_088.title: The Old Tongue Lives
text: At Veya's suggestion, you begin holding language sessions in the safe house — teaching the old dark tongue to exiles who've forgotten it and surface-born dark elves who never learned it. The sessions are awkward at first. The old tongue was designed for the warrens' acoustics — its sibilants carry through stone corridors in ways that the surface world's open spaces distort. Speaking it in the server farm feels like singing in a closet.

But the language adapts. The exiles develop a surface dialect — the same vocabulary and grammar, but with pronunciation shifts that account for the different acoustic environment. The sibilants soften. The gutturals sharpen. The language evolves, as languages do, to fit the mouths and ears of the people who use it.

The Matriarch would call this corruption. You call it survival. A language that can't change is a language that dies. A culture that can't evolve is a culture that fossilizes. The dark elves have spent millennia in unchanging warrens, perfecting traditions that hardened into prisons. Maybe the surface, with its chaos and its change, is exactly what the old tongue needed.

By the third session, Thessaly is making jokes in the old tongue. Wraith is composing haiku. Ven has started writing technical documentation in dark script. The language lives. It changes. It grows. Like everything else on the surface, it finds a way.

choices.text: Speak the old tongue. Keep it alive in a world that forgets.

delf_089.title: The Night Before
text: The night before you go to Mako's, you sit in your maintenance corridor and take stock. Equipment: one kin-blade, one obsidian shard mirror, one surveillance kit, one clean datapad. Skills: Weaver-class pattern recognition, Blade-class combat, Shadow-class concealment, Sigil-class shadow arts including the forbidden Third Shadow. Contacts: the exile coalition, the surface information network, Veya, Kael'ris if he's still above ground.
Resources: minimal. Reputation: growing. Purpose: clear.

You are a dark elf without a House, in a city that doesn't understand what dark elves are. You are fighting a corporation that wants to replace your species with a product line. You are building a community from the wreckage of a culture that was designed to prevent community from forming. And you are heading to a meeting at a repair shop in Sector 7, summoned by an unknown contact who claims to have intelligence about the two greatest threats to your existence.

The Matriarch would calculate the risk-reward ratio and make a strategic decision. You're done calculating. You're going because the thread is there and you want to see where it leads. Because for the first time in your life, you're pulling your own threads, weaving your own pattern, living a story that no Matriarch authored.
You slide the obsidian mirror back into your pocket, draw the First Shadow, and step into the night.

choices.text: The night before is for planning, not sentiment.

delf_090.title: The Coalition Gathers
text: A final gathering before the crossroads. The exile coalition meets in the safe house — all of them, for the first time since the crisis. Thessaly, Wraith, Ven, Mira, Sel, the two independents. Veya. Kael'ris, if he's joined you. Seven to nine dark elves, sitting in a circle in a dead server farm beneath a transit hub, planning a future that the Houses would declare impossible.

You brief them on the Mako's contact. Unknown source, unknown motivations, potentially valuable intelligence on Eclipse and the Matriarch. You're going. The question is whether you go alone or with support.
The vote is unanimous. Support. 'You don't walk into unknown territory without backup,' Wraith says. 'Even the Matriarch knew that.'
'The Matriarch's backup was an army,' you say.
'Her army was compelled. We're volunteers. Better.' He grins, the sharp-toothed grin of a dark elf who's found something to fight for besides obligation.

You look at the faces around you. Exiles. Outcasts. The broken pieces of a culture that was never designed to break. And yet here they are, choosing to be together, choosing to fight, choosing to build something from the wreckage that looks nothing like what was destroyed and might — might — be better.
The crossroads awaits.

choices.text: [Charisma 11] Unite the coalition. Speak the words that bind.

delf_091.title: The Darkness Within
text: A final meditation in the deep tunnels. You draw the Third Shadow and let the Deep Dark fill you, and this time you don't fight it. You let it carry you to the edge of the Void Echo, and you listen.

The pattern is there. The vast intelligence in the deep places, ancient and patient and incomprehensibly complex. It doesn't speak — speech implies the limitations of language. It communicates in patterns, in the mathematical poetry of connections and relationships that a Weaver's mind is uniquely equipped to receive.

It says, in patterns that translate loosely to concepts: the dark is not empty. The dark is not hostile. The dark is not the absence of light. The dark is the space where possibilities exist before they are observed, where patterns form before they are recognized, where the future takes shape in the quantum gaps between moments.

Dark elves knew this, once. Before the Houses, before the politics, before the centuries of accumulated cruelty. They chose the dark because the dark chose them — because their biology, their neurology, their fundamental nature resonated with the space between photons in a way that no other species could match.
You carry the darkness. Not as a weapon or a tool. As a heritage. The deepest, oldest part of what it means to be a dark elf.
You open your eyes. The crossroads is waiting.

choices.text: The darkness within is a tool, not a curse. Use it.

delf_092.title: Veya's Farewell
text: Veya finds you at the Glass Eye one last time before the crossroads. She's wearing her own face — no Shadow-mask, no persona, just Veya. She orders two drinks and pushes one across the table.

'Whatever happens at Mako's,' she says, 'I want you to know that you saved me. Not from the warrens. From myself. When I couldn't find my own face, you reminded me it existed. That's not a Weaver's skill. That's a sibling's.'
You drink. The surface alcohol is still terrible by warren standards, but it's grown on you.

'I've been thinking about the Matriarch,' Veya continues. 'About how she raised us. The Crucible, the conditioning, the deliberate erosion of attachment. She was building weapons. But weapons don't work if they develop opinions. We developed opinions. That's why she lost us.'
'We lost her too,' you say.

'No.' Veya shakes her head. 'We never had her. You can't lose something that was never offered. She gave us skills, not love. Training, not home. The home we built ourselves, here, in this terrible city with its terrible drinks and its terrible light. That's ours. Nobody gave it to us. Nobody can take it away.'
She raises her glass. 'To the exile coalition. Population: growing.'
You raise yours. 'To the new dark.'

choices.text: Veya's farewell is not goodbye. It's 'see you in the shadows.'

delf_093.title: The Pattern Complete
text: Standing in the rain outside Mako's Repair Shop, you see the weave. Not just the tactical picture. Not just the power structures and information flows. The whole thing. The complete pattern of your life, from the Crucible to the exile, from the warrens to the surface, from the Matriarch's instrument to a free agent standing in acid rain with an obsidian blade and a network of chosen kin.

Every thread connects. The Crucible gave you discipline. The exile gave you freedom. The warrens gave you the shadow arts. The surface gave you purpose. The Matriarch gave you the Weaver's eye. You gave yourself the right to use it as you see fit.
The door to Mako's is ahead. Beyond it, the next thread. The next pattern. The next chapter of a story that has already exceeded anything the Matriarch planned or the Crucible anticipated.
You are a dark elf on the surface. You are a Weaver without a loom. You are the exile who built a new home in the ruins of the old. And you are ready.
Time to see what's on the other side of the door.

choices.text: [Intelligence 10] Analyze the completed pattern. What does it reveal?

delf_094.title: Shadow Protocol
text: Before heading to Mako's, you run a full shadow protocol. Sweep your maintenance corridor for surveillance devices — clean. Check the dead drops for messages — nothing new. Update the exile coalition's secure comm channel with your location and estimated timeline — Veya acknowledges, Wraith confirms backup position.

The protocol is second nature. The Crucible drilled it into you until it was automatic, and the surface hasn't dulled it. If anything, surface operations have sharpened it — the threat environment is less controlled, more variable, requiring constant adaptation that the warrens' predictable architecture never demanded.

Equipment check. Kin-blade, concealed. Shadow-cloak, functional. Comm patch, live. Data crystal with Eclipse intelligence, secured in a hidden pocket. Obsidian mirror, because you've come to rely on it for reasons that have nothing to do with vanity.

You are as prepared as you can be for an unknown situation. The Weaver's paradox: you plan for everything, knowing that nothing goes according to plan. The plan is not the point. The capacity to adapt when the plan fails — that's the point.
Ready.

choices.text: Go to Mako's. But go through the tunnels, not the streets.

delf_095.title: The Exile's Creed
text: On the wall of the safe house, someone — you think it was Thessaly, but no one has claimed it — has written a phrase in the old dark tongue. The script is careful, deliberate, using the traditional character forms that took centuries to develop in the warrens' stone-carved signage. It reads: 'Val'thir syn moreth — the shadow serves the light it has chosen.'

It's a corruption of the House Vel'tharin motto — 'Val'thir syn moth,' 'the shadow serves the throne.' One letter changed. One word replaced. And the meaning inverts entirely: from obedience to choice. From serving power to serving purpose.

The exile coalition has adopted it as their creed. Not formally — dark elves don't do formal declarations. But informally, in the way that matters: by acting on it. Every operation, every decision, every risk taken in the service of protecting the dark elf community from Eclipse and the Matriarch's sell-off — all of it flows from the simple idea that shadow is not a weapon. Shadow is a choice.
You touch the words on the wall. The old script feels right under your fingers, even carved into industrial concrete instead of obsidian. The medium changes. The message endures.

choices.text: The exile's creed: survive, adapt, overcome.

delf_096.title: One Last Thread
text: Before Mako's, one last piece of business. You visit the dead drop where the Matriarch's message arrived. You leave a response in the family cipher — three words, the same three words Kael'ris used when you faced each other across the barricade in the eastern corridor.
'We are kin.'

Not House. Not bloodline. Kin. The word that means chosen. The word that means: I do not serve you, I do not obey you, but I do not hate you. I understand what you are. I understand what made you. I reject the system you built, but I acknowledge the person who built it. You were wrong. You were also, in your way, trying to survive. I can hold both truths at once because you taught me to see all threads, even the ones that hurt.

The Matriarch may never read it. The dead drop may be monitored. The cipher may be broken. None of that matters. The words are not for her. They're for you. A closing. An acknowledgment. A thread tied off before the new weave begins.
You walk away from the dead drop and into the rain and toward Mako's Repair Shop and toward the rest of your life.

choices.text: [Wisdom 10] Pull the last thread with care. The whole weave depends on it.

delf_097.title: The Assassin's Beginning
text: You choose the Blade's path — not the Crucible's version, bound by House loyalty and the Matriarch's commands, but a free blade. A weapon that aims itself. The skills are the same: shadow-step, concealment, the precise application of force to critical vulnerabilities. But the purpose is yours.

The city has threats that need cutting. Meridian's extraction teams. Eclipse's research personnel. The corporate agents who hunt your people for their genetics. Each one is a thread that, pulled correctly, unravels a piece of the weave that threatens your species. You will be the one who pulls them.

You check the kin-blade at your hip. The obsidian edge catches no light — it absorbs it, the way dark elves absorb the dark. A weapon made from the same volcanic glass as the warrens' walls. A connection to the deep places, carried on the surface.
Veya's voice on the comm: 'Mako's is clean. No surveillance, no traps. Whatever's in there, it's genuine.'

You draw the First Shadow, become one with the darkness between the streetlights, and move toward the repair shop. The Blade walks. The city sleeps. And somewhere in the deep tunnels, the exile coalition stands ready to catch you if you fall.
The crossroads opens. A new story begins.

choices.text: Step into the light. The assassin's blade is ready.

delf_098.title: The Spymaster's Beginning
text: You choose the Weaver's path — the true Weaver's path, freed from the Matriarch's restrictions. A spymaster without a throne. An intelligence director whose only agenda is the survival and sovereignty of a species that the world is trying to commodify.

The network is your weapon. Every contact, every data stream, every whispered confidence in a bar or maintenance corridor — all of it feeds the pattern. And you sit at the center, not as a ruler but as a reader, translating the vast complexity of the city's power structures into actionable intelligence for the exile coalition.

Eclipse. The Matriarch. The Houses. Meridian. The city government. Each one a thread in a weave that determines whether dark elves will survive as a free species or be replaced by corporate products. You see all the threads. You see where they cross and where they tangle and where they fray. And you know — with the cold, absolute clarity that the Crucible gave you and the surface refined — exactly which threads to pull.

The datapad shows Mako's address. The unknown contact waits. A new thread enters the weave, and the pattern shifts in ways you haven't predicted. That's fine. The Weaver doesn't need to predict everything. The Weaver needs to adapt faster than the pattern changes.
You've been adapting your entire life. One more adaptation won't break you.
The crossroads opens. The Weaver walks.

choices.text: Information is the sharpest weapon. Wield it.

delf_099.title: The Rogue Scholar's Beginning
text: You choose the Sigil's path — but not the sheltered, authorized research of the warrens. The rogue scholar. The keeper of forbidden knowledge, studying the shadow arts and the Deep Dark and the Void Echo not for a House's benefit but for the species' understanding.

The Third Shadow. The Deep Dark. The Void Echo. These are not just weapons or tools — they are windows into the fundamental nature of reality. The dark elves discovered them through millennia of experimentation in the deep places, and the Houses classified them as military assets. But they are more than military assets. They are knowledge. Pure, dangerous, irreplaceable knowledge about the nature of darkness and its relationship to light, to matter, to consciousness.

If Eclipse creates synthetic dark elves with shadow affinity, it will be using this knowledge as an engineering specification. A recipe to be followed. The synthetic operatives will have the abilities without the understanding. They'll wield the Third Shadow without knowing what the Third Shadow is.

You will be the one who ensures that the understanding survives. The one who studies, records, teaches. Not in the warrens' controlled curriculum, but freely, openly, to anyone with the aptitude and the will.
Knowledge wants to be free. Even knowledge from the dark.
The crossroads opens. The Scholar walks toward the light, carrying the darkness inside.

choices.text: Knowledge is power. The scholar's path begins.

delf_100.title: The Shadowbroker's Beginning
text: You choose the path that combines all three — Blade, Shadow, Sigil — into something the Houses never intended. The shadowbroker. Not a servant of power but a dealer in it. Information, influence, access — the currencies of the dark, traded on the open market by someone who understands their true value.

The Matriarch built an empire by controlling the flow of information. You will build something different: a marketplace. A place where secrets are bought and sold on terms that benefit the community, not the throne. Where dark elf intelligence skills are a service, not a weapon. Where the deep knowledge of shadow and silence is available to anyone who needs it and can pay fair price.

It's capitalism applied to espionage, and it's the most un-dark-elf idea you've ever had. The Houses would be appalled. The Matriarch would call it vulgar. But the surface world runs on transactions, and if the dark elves are going to survive on the surface, they need to learn to trade in its currencies.

You'll build the network. You'll set the prices. You'll ensure that the dark elf community benefits from the skills that the Houses hoarded and the Matriarch sold for personal gain. Power through secrets, but shared power. Distributed power. The kind that no single Matriarch can control.
The crossroads opens. The Shadowbroker steps into the rain, carrying the weight of an empire that doesn't exist yet but will.
Your story continues. The message from Mako's awaits.

choices.text: The shadows are your domain. Trade in them.

delf_014_fail.title: Detected in the Chaos
text: Drone infrared catches your heat signature. Spotlight pins you.
Being seen is worse than being hit. Cover blown.

choices.text: Freeze. Wait for the light to pass.

delf_016_fail.title: Force Without Finesse
text: Dark elf strength is borrowed. Your blow barely staggers them.
Exposed for nothing. Force isn't yours.

delf_020_fail.title: The Weave Resists
text: Analysis loops back. Maze of false endpoints.
Hours wasted. No weaknesses found.

delf_029_fail.title: Sprung Your Own Trap
text: Tripwire. Flash-bang sears dark-adapted eyes.
Blind, ears ringing, senses stripped.

choices.text: Even impaired, still a dark elf.

delf_030_fail.title: Overthinking
text: Perfect plan. Window closed by the time it's done.
Reality didn't wait.

delf_031_fail.title: The Tunnel Echoes
text: Acoustics amplify every step. Making sound means failure.
Something ahead heard you.

delf_046_fail.title: The Trace Finds You
text: Biometric scanner recorded you. Signature broadcasting.
Anonymity compromised.

delf_047_fail.title: Encryption Failure
text: Proprietary format. Cipher crashes halfway.
Alert triggered. They know your priorities.

delf_048_fail.title: Tunnels Compromised
text: Fresh motion sensors. You trigger one. Alarm shrieks.
Emergency lights turn sanctuary into stage.

delf_050_fail.title: Coalition Fractures
text: Clinical delivery where passion needed. Shadow asking for trust.
Coalition fractures. Proof nobody acts on.

delf_052_fail.title: Identity Cracks
text: Biometric references a closed clinic. First check reveals the gap.
Mask with a crack. Every checkpoint a gamble.

delf_054_fail.title: Network Breach
text: One contact already compromised. Three safe houses burned.
Network contracts instead of expanding.

delf_056_fail.title: Force Meets Resistance
text: Dark elves work best alone. Synchronized attack drifts apart.
Surgical strike became butcher's work.

delf_057_fail.title: Spin Unravels
text: Story spread too fast through uncontrolled channels.
Fallout lands on you.

delf_058_fail.title: Blindsided
text: They weaponized public opinion instead.
Wrong horizon.

delf_062_fail.title: Wrong Thread Pulled
text: Wrong thread tightens the knot.
Allies exposed. Resources gone.

delf_066_fail.title: Test Reveals Nothing
text: He passes. A disloyal agent would know to pass.
Wasted resources proving your own distrust.

delf_071_fail.title: The Echo Betrays
text: Dampening panels swallow echoes. Navigating blind.
Dead ends. Warren became cage.

delf_074_fail.title: Power Obscured
text: Redundancies. Weak points are decoys.
Designed to waste your time.

delf_077_fail.title: The Mirror's Distortion
text: Mirror shows only your hollow-eyed face.
Confirmation of how lost you are.

delf_079_fail.title: Miscalculated Cost
text: Math was perfect. Some costs can't be calculated.
Being right makes it worse.

delf_081_fail.title: Web Snapped
text: Someone cut your web. Three contacts dark.
Dismantled by someone who knows dark elf networks.

delf_082_fail.title: The Night Has Eyes
text: Infrared surveillance designed to counter you. Rubber bullet hits wall.
The night no longer belongs to you alone.

delf_084_fail.title: Warning Misread
text: Real warning hidden in what Wraith didn't say.
Guarded the wrong door.

delf_087_fail.title: Reflection Without Clarity
text: Rain washes grime, not doubt.
Cold, wet, and no wiser.

delf_090_fail.title: Words That Divide
text: Coalition needs passion. Dark elves trained it out.
Speech ends in uncomfortable silence.

delf_093_fail.title: Pattern Without Meaning
text: Data clear. Implications not.
Perfect map of unknown territory.

delf_096_fail.title: The Weave Unravels
text: Thread snags. Pattern distorts. Careful work collapses.
The last thread was holding everything.
